docs.title: Example: DMA and Interrupt Logic
docs.text: LabWired models Direct Memory Access (DMA) and External Interrupts (EXTI) using a deterministic two-phase execution cycle. This ensures that concurrent hardware events (like memory transfers and IRQ assertion) occur in a predictable order relative to CPU instruction execution.

docs.title: 1. DMA Memory-to-Memory Transfer
docs.text: The DMA controller processes transfer requests at the end of the CPU instruction cycle.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
To initiate a memory-to-memory transfer, the firmware must configure the DMA channel with the MEM2MEM bit set.

// DMA1 Channel 1 Configuration
fn configure_dma_transfer(src: u32, dest: u32, len: u16) {
    let dma = unsafe { &*DMA1::ptr() };

    // 1. Source and Destination Addresses
    dma.ch1.cpar.write(|w| unsafe { w.bits(src) });
    dma.ch1.cmar.write(|w| unsafe { w.bits(dest) });

    // 2. Transfer Length
    dma.ch1.cndtr.write(|w| unsafe { w.bits(len as u32) });

    // 3. Control Register (Enable, Mem2Mem, Increment Ptrs)
    dma.ch1.ccr.write(|w| w
        .mem2mem().set_bit() 
        .pl().very_high()
        .minc().enabled()
        .pinc().enabled()
        .en().enabled()
    );
}

docs.title: Execution Model
docs.text:
1. Request Phase: When CCR.EN is set, the DMA controller registers a pending request internally. It does not immediately modify memory.
  2. Bus Arbitration: At the end of the current CPU cycle, the SystemBus polls the DMA controller.
  3. Transfer Phase: The bus executes the memory copy operation (read from source, write to destination).

docs.title: 2. External Interrupts (EXTI)
docs.text: External signals are routed through the EXTI controller to the Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC).

docs.title: Stimulus Configuration
docs.text:
To verify interrupt logic, use a test script to assert a GPIO pin state.

System Manifest (system.yaml): 

chip: "../chips/stm32f103.yaml"
inputs:
  - id: "user_button"
    pin: "PA0"
    mode: "PushPull"

Test Script (test_interrupts.yaml): 

steps:
  - run: 100ms
  - set_pin: 
      pin: "PA0"
      state: "high"
  - run: 1us
  - assert_interrupt: "EXTI0"

docs.title: Signal Propagation
docs.text:
1. GPIO: The pin state change is detected by the GPIO peripheral.
  2. AFIO: The signal is routed to the corresponding EXTI line (e.g., PA0 -> EXTI0) based on the AFIO_EXTICR configuration.
  3. EXTI: The controller detects the rising/falling edge and sets the Pending Register (PR) bit.
  4. NVIC: The interrupt is forwarded to the NVIC. If the priority acts, the CPU vectors to the ISR on the next instruction fetch.

docs.title: Example: I2C Sensor Simulation
docs.text: This example demonstrates how to simulate an I2C peripheral (e.g., TMP102) and verify its behavior using standard Rust firmware.

docs.title: 1. System Configuration
docs.text: The simulation environment requires defining the sensor in the system manifest and connecting it to the appropriate I2C controller.

docs.title: Configuration (system.yaml)
docs.text:
chip: "../chips/stm32f103.yaml"
peripherals:
  - id: "i2c1"
    type: "i2c_master"
    base_address: 0x40005400

  - id: "tmp102"
    type: "i2c_temp_sensor"  # Instantiates the TMP102 model
    address: 0x48            # 7-bit I2C address
    bus: "i2c1"              # Connects to the I2C1 controller

docs.title: 2. Firmware Integration
docs.text: The firmware uses standard HAL calls to interact with the simulated device. No simulation-specific code is required in the firmware itself.

docs.title: Rust Implementation (stm32f1xx-hal)
docs.text:
use stm32f1xx_hal::{i2c::{BlockingI2c, Mode}, pac};

fn main() -> ! {
    let dp = pac::Peripherals::take().unwrap();
    // ... Clock Configuration ...

    // Initialize I2C1 (Standard Mode, 100kHz)
    let mut i2c = BlockingI2c::i2c1(
        dp.I2C1,
        (scl, sda), // Pins PB6, PB7
        &mut afio.mapr,
        Mode::Standard { frequency: 100.kHz() },
        clocks,
        &mut rcc.apb1,
        1000, 10, 1000, 1000,
    );

    let sensor_addr = 0x48;
    let mut buffer = [0u8; 2];

    loop {
        // Read Temperature Register (0x00)
        i2c.write_read(sensor_addr, &[0x00], &mut buffer).unwrap();

        // Convert to Celsius (12-bit, 0.0625°C resolution)
        let raw_temp = u16::from_be_bytes(buffer) >> 4;
        let celsius = raw_temp as f32 * 0.0625;
    }
}

docs.title: 3. Automated Verification
docs.text: LabWired supports scripted fault injection to verify error handling logic.

docs.title: Fault Injection Script (tests/fault_test.yaml)
docs.text:
Simulates a sensor failure or extreme environmental condition.

steps:
  - run: 100ms
  - write_peripheral:
      id: "tmp102"
      reg: "TEMP"
      value: 0x7FF0 # Force sensor reading to 128°C
  - run: 10ms
  - assert_log: "CRITICAL: OVERTEMP DETECTED"

docs.title: 4. Execution
docs.text:
Run the simulation with the test script:

labwired test --script tests/fault_test.yaml

The simulator will execute the firmware, inject the fault at the specified time, and verify that the firmware correctly detects and logs the error condition.

docs.title: Walkthrough: Integrated STM32 Test Example
docs.text: This example demonstrates how to use the labwired test runner to verify a real STM32 firmware on a custom system configuration.

docs.title: Chip Descriptor (stm32f103.yaml)
docs.text: Defines the memory map and peripherals (DMA, EXTI, AFIO, UART).

docs.title: System Manifest (stm32f103-integrated-test.yaml)
docs.text: Wires the chip into a specific system context. In this case, it just uses the raw chip.

docs.title: Firmware (main.rs)
docs.text: A Rust no_std firmware that: - Performs 32-bit division. - Configures DMA1 Channel 1 for a memory-to-memory transfer. - Triggers a software interrupt via EXTI Line 0. - Enters an infinite loop.

docs.title: 2. The Test Script
docs.text:
The test script (stm32f103_integrated_test.yaml) automates the simulation:

schema_version: "1.0"
inputs:
  firmware: "../../target/thumbv7m-none-eabi/debug/firmware"
  system: "../../configs/systems/stm32f103-integrated-test.yaml"
limits:
  max_steps: 10000
  no_progress_steps: 1000
assertions:
  - expected_stop_reason: no_progress

docs.title: 3. Running the Test
docs.text:
Run the test locally using the CLI:

labwired test --script examples/tests/stm32f103_integrated_test.yaml --output-dir out/test-results

docs.title: Inspecting Results
docs.text: The runner generates several artifacts in out/test-results: - result.json: Summary of execution stats and assertion results. - snapshot.json: Full state of the CPU and peripherals at the end of the run. - uart.log: Captured data from the UART peripheral.

docs.title: 4. CI Integration
docs.text:
You can easily integrate this into a GitHub Actions workflow:

- name: Run LabWired Integrated Test
  run: |
    labwired test \
      --script examples/tests/stm32f103_integrated_test.yaml \
      --output-dir artifacts

docs.title: SoC factory migration
docs.text:
Goal: every chip builds through the data-driven SystemBus::from_config path (walk the chip YAML → per-family factory → place at base), with arch code shrinking to a thin CPU-core overlay. Models stay statically linked; topology becomes data. Today the Cortex-M and RISC-V chips already build this way; the ESP32-S3 is the lone holdout, assembled by the 2186-line hand-wired system::xtensa::configure_xtensa_esp32s3.

Strangler migration, each stage gated by the ESP32/Xtensa test suite as a golden oracle (no behavior change until Stage 3):

  • Stage 0 — pin the oracle. Baseline pass/fail of the ESP32/Xtensa test binaries (321 tests). See STAGE0_golden_baseline.txt.
  • Stage 1 — pre-stock the factory. peripherals/esp32s3/factory.rs::try_build hosts the 26 ESP32-S3 peripheral types; from_config delegates to it before the generic match. Pure addition — no chip YAML references these types yet, so behavior is unchanged (regression identical to Stage 0).
  • Stage 2 — separate core from peripherals. configure_xtensa_esp32s3 is now three parts: configure_esp32s3_memmap (RAM/flash-XIP/MMU/ROM), the 12 core registrations inline (intmatrix, crosscore_ipi, core1_control, extmem, spimem1, system/system_regs/system_regs_hi, rtc_cntl, efuse, low_mmio, mmio_rest), and register_esp32s3_peripherals (all peripheral models — the unit Stage 3 deletes). Done in verbatim, behavior-preserving moves; routing is address-pure (greatest-start-wins), with UARTs kept after the catch-alls for the one same-base tie. Verified each step against brom_boot_smoke + firmware_survival. ← current
  • Stage 3 — flip esp32s3 to from_config. Complete esp32s3.yaml (all peripherals, using the esp32s3_* type strings + per-instance irq), build the peripherals via from_config, keep the core inline as a configure_xtensa_core overlay, diff against the golden, then delete register_esp32s3_peripherals.
  • Stage 4 — repeat for esp32 / esp32c3.
  • Stage 5 — feature-gate families (cortex-m/xtensa/esp32/nrf52) so the wasm build and CI fixtures compile only what they need.

docs.title: Pattern
docs.text: Per-family factories live in their own module (peripherals/<family>/factory.rs), not inline in bus::from_config. This keeps the central match from growing and lets it shrink as other families (nRF52, STM32) move out the same way.

docs.title: Plan 1.5 — HW Runner Cleanup and Sim-Side TODO Closure — Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For agentic workers: REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (- [ ]) syntax for tracking.

Goal: Close Plan 1 exit criterion 4 (90/90 _hw/_diff variants pass on the connected ESP32-S3-Zero) and close the four documented sim-side gaps from docs/case_study_esp32s3_plan1.md §"Known Gaps and Acknowledged Limitations".

Architecture: Nine independent fixes across two crates (crates/hw-oracle/ and crates/core/src/cpu/). The HW-runner fixes refactor crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs and crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs to add SR dispatch, windowed-register translation, VECBASE write sequencing, and sub-word DRAM readback workarounds. The sim-side fixes touch four narrow sites in crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs. Every fix lands as its own commit with a dedicated #[hw_oracle_test] per the §7.5 methodology rule from the design spec.

Tech Stack: Rust 2024 edition, OpenOCD TCL via TCP socket (port 6666), xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as from ~/.rustup/toolchains/esp/xtensa-esp-elf/esp-15.2.0_20250920/, ESP32-S3-Zero board (USB-JTAG 303a:1001), goblin for ELF parsing.

Spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-25-plan-1.5-hw-runner-and-sim-cleanup.md

docs.title: Pre-flight
docs.text: The work happens on a new branch off the current Plan 1 foundation. The board must be physically connected before HW oracle tests can run.

docs.title: Task 0: Branch + baseline verification
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: (no files; git branch + verification only)

  • [ ] Step 1: Confirm clean working tree on the foundation branch
git status
git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD

Expected output: nothing to commit, working tree clean and feature/esp32s3-plan1-foundation.

  • [ ] Step 2: Create the Plan 1.5 branch
git checkout -b feature/esp32s3-plan1.5-cleanup

Expected: Switched to a new branch 'feature/esp32s3-plan1.5-cleanup'.

  • [ ] Step 3: Capture baseline test counts
cargo test --workspace \
  --exclude firmware \
  --exclude firmware-ci-fixture \
  --exclude riscv-ci-fixture \
  2>&1 | tail -20

Expected: ≥ 461 sim tests passing across the workspace. Record the exact count (e.g., "461 passed; 0 failed") in a scratch note for end-of-plan diff. This is the "sim baseline".

  • [ ] Step 4: Capture HW oracle baseline
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 \
  2>&1 | tail -30

Expected: 62/90 _hw + _diff variants pass. Record the exact count and the names of any new failures/passes for diff at end of plan.

  • [ ] Step 5: Capture baseline of #[hw_oracle_test] count
grep -c "#\[hw_oracle_test\]" crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs

Expected: 46. This is the baseline for exit-criterion 4 (must reach ≥ 55 by end of plan).

  • [ ] Step 6: Confirm xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as is on PATH
which xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as && xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as --version | head -1

Expected: a path under ~/.rustup/toolchains/esp/xtensa-esp-elf/esp-15.2.0_20250920/bin/ and a version string. If missing, add the bin directory to PATH for this shell session before continuing.

docs.title: Task 1: SR access form dispatch (HW root cause #1)
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs:177-193 (add SR dispatch alongside existing read_register/write_register) - Modify: crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs:1080-1111 (route the SR setup loop in capture_hw_state through the new dispatch)

Background: The current oc.read_register("epc1") issues reg epc1 to OpenOCD. On the ESP32-S3 LX7 target some SR readbacks return 0 because the correct command form is xtensa rsr epc1 (or another variant). This task adds a per-SR access-form probe and caches the working form.

  • [ ] Step 1: Manually probe SR access forms on the live board

Spawn OpenOCD interactively and try each form for a couple of representative SRs (epc1, vecbase, exccause):

killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg epc1" \
  -c "xtensa rsr epc1" \
  -c "ocd_reg epc1" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -20

Expected: at least one of the three commands prints a hex value. Record which form works for each of epc1, epc2, vecbase, exccause, eps2, excsave1, intenable, interrupt, sar, scompare1 in a scratch note. Most likely either reg <name> or xtensa rsr <name> will be the correct form across all SRs.

  • [ ] Step 2: Write the failing test (SR readback regression)

Append a new oracle test to crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs at the bottom of the H8 section (after vecbase_relocation_oracle at line 1371):

// ── H8.7: SR readback regression (Plan 1.5 fix #1) ──────────────────────────

/// Setup writes a sentinel into EPC1 and the SR dispatch on capture_hw_state
/// must read it back identically.  The Plan-1 runner returned 0 here because
/// `reg epc1` was the wrong OpenOCD command form for this SR.
///
/// Program: a single BREAK so execution halts immediately.
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn epc1_setup_readback_oracle() -> OracleCase {
    OracleCase::from_bytes(vec![])
        .setup(|st| {
            st.write_epc1(0xDEAD_BEEF);
        })
        .expect(|st| {
            st.assert_epc1(0xDEAD_BEEF);
        })
}
  • [ ] Step 3: Run the new test on sim and HW to confirm the failure mode
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle epc1_setup_readback_oracle_sim

Expected: PASS (sim path already correct).

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  epc1_setup_readback_oracle_hw -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: FAIL with EPC1: expected 0xDEADBEEF, got 0x00000000 (or similar). This confirms the bug.

  • [ ] Step 4: Add the SR dispatch enum and probe to openocd.rs

Insert the following just above the existing read_register method (around line 178 of crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs):

    // ── SR access form dispatch (Plan 1.5 fix #1) ─────────────────────────────

    /// One of three OpenOCD command forms for accessing an Xtensa SR.
    ///
    /// The ESP32-S3 LX7 target exposes some SRs only via `xtensa rsr <name>`;
    /// others are exposed as ordinary `reg <name>` aliases.  We probe each form
    /// once per session per SR and cache the working one.
    #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
    pub enum SrAccessForm {
        /// `reg <name>` — works for general-purpose registers and most SRs.
        Reg,
        /// `xtensa rsr <name>` / `xtensa wsr <name> <val>` — Xtensa-specific.
        XtensaRsrWsr,
    }

    /// Probe which OpenOCD command form works for SR `name`.
    ///
    /// Tries `reg <name>` first, then `xtensa rsr <name>`.  Returns the first
    /// form whose response parses to a hex value; returns an error if none work.
    pub fn probe_sr_form(&mut self, name: &str) -> Result<SrAccessForm> {
        // Try `reg <name>` first.
        if let Ok(resp) = self.tcl(&format!("reg {}", name)) {
            if parse_hex_from_response(&resp).is_ok() {
                return Ok(SrAccessForm::Reg);
            }
        }
        // Try `xtensa rsr <name>`.
        if let Ok(resp) = self.tcl(&format!("xtensa rsr {}", name)) {
            if parse_hex_from_response(&resp).is_ok() {
                return Ok(SrAccessForm::XtensaRsrWsr);
            }
        }
        Err(anyhow!("probe_sr_form: no working command form for SR '{name}'"))
    }

    /// Read SR `name` using the cached or probed access form.
    ///
    /// On first call for a given SR, probes both forms via `probe_sr_form`.
    /// Subsequent calls reuse the cached form.
    pub fn read_sr(&mut self, name: &str) -> Result<u32> {
        let form = self.sr_form(name)?;
        match form {
            SrAccessForm::Reg => self.read_register(name),
            SrAccessForm::XtensaRsrWsr => {
                let resp = self.tcl(&format!("xtensa rsr {}", name))?;
                parse_hex_from_response(&resp)
                    .with_context(|| format!("read_sr({name}) xtensa rsr: {resp:?}"))
            }
        }
    }

    /// Write `val` to SR `name` using the cached or probed access form.
    pub fn write_sr(&mut self, name: &str, val: u32) -> Result<()> {
        let form = self.sr_form(name)?;
        match form {
            SrAccessForm::Reg => self.write_register(name, val),
            SrAccessForm::XtensaRsrWsr => {
                self.tcl(&format!("xtensa wsr {} 0x{:08x}", name, val))?;
                Ok(())
            }
        }
    }

    /// Internal: return the cached or freshly probed `SrAccessForm` for `name`.
    fn sr_form(&mut self, name: &str) -> Result<SrAccessForm> {
        if let Some(&form) = self.sr_forms.get(name) {
            return Ok(form);
        }
        let form = self.probe_sr_form(name)?;
        self.sr_forms.insert(name.to_string(), form);
        Ok(form)
    }

Add the cache field to the OpenOcd struct definition (around line 14):

pub struct OpenOcd {
    child: Child,
    sock: TcpStream,
    /// Cache of probed SR access forms, keyed by SR name.
    sr_forms: std::collections::HashMap<String, SrAccessForm>,
}

Initialize the cache in both spawn_with_args constructors. In spawn_with_args (around line 111), change:

        Ok(Self { child, sock })

to:

        Ok(Self {
            child,
            sock,
            sr_forms: std::collections::HashMap::new(),
        })
  • [ ] Step 5: Route SR access in capture_hw_state through the new dispatch

In crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs around line 970-975, replace:

    for (&sr_id, &val) in &init_state.sr {
        let sr_name = sr_id_to_openocd_name(sr_id);
        if let Some(name) = sr_name {
            let _ = oc.write_register(name, val);
        }
    }

with:

    for (&sr_id, &val) in &init_state.sr {
        let sr_name = sr_id_to_openocd_name(sr_id);
        if let Some(name) = sr_name {
            oc.write_sr(name, val)
                .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: write_sr({name}, 0x{val:08X}) failed: {e}"));
        }
    }

And in the end-state capture (around lines 1057-1058), replace:

    end.epc1 = oc.read_register("epc1").unwrap_or(0);
    end.exccause = oc.read_register("exccause").unwrap_or(0);

with:

    end.epc1 = oc.read_sr("epc1").unwrap_or(0);
    end.exccause = oc.read_sr("exccause").unwrap_or(0);
  • [ ] Step 6: Run the failing test on HW; expect PASS
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  epc1_setup_readback_oracle -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: both epc1_setup_readback_oracle_hw and epc1_setup_readback_oracle_diff PASS.

  • [ ] Step 7: Confirm sim baseline holds
cargo test --workspace \
  --exclude firmware \
  --exclude firmware-ci-fixture \
  --exclude riscv-ci-fixture \
  2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: ≥ 462 passing (baseline 461 + 1 new sim test).

  • [ ] Step 8: Confirm full HW oracle bank does not regress
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: at least 62 + 1 = 63 passing variants, plus likely a few more (any test that depended on epc1 readback in its expect closure now passes too — most notably exccause_epc1_readback_oracle_hw). Record new total.

  • [ ] Step 9: Commit
git add crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs
git commit -m "fix(hw-oracle): SR access form dispatch (read_sr/write_sr)

Plan 1.5 fix #1: some Xtensa SRs require 'xtensa rsr <name>' rather
than 'reg <name>' on the ESP32-S3 LX7 target. New SrAccessForm enum +
per-session probe + cache routes each SR access through the working
form. Adds epc1_setup_readback_oracle as a regression."
git push -u origin feature/esp32s3-plan1.5-cleanup

docs.title: Task 2: Windowed-register translation (HW root cause #2)
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs (add read_logical_ar / write_logical_ar) - Modify: crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs:921-994 (route AR writes through the new layer + reorder so WB is set first)

Background: When a test calls st.write_windowbase(4), the runner zeroes registers (lines 921-929) with WB=0 (still the boot default), then writes the test's setup registers (lines 964-967) — also still with WB=0 — and only then sets WB=4 (line 977-979). After that point, read_register("a3") reads physical AR15 (because WB=4, and a3 = (4*4 + 3) & 0x3F = 19). The setup writes to a3 went to physical AR3, which is now inaccessible without changing WB. Result: every windowed-test where init_windowbase is non-zero sees garbage in its setup registers.

The fix has two parts: 1. Reorder operations in capture_hw_state so WB is set BEFORE register writes that depend on it. 2. Add explicit logical AR helpers in openocd.rs that read current WB and route through it. This is for end-state capture, where reading a0..a15 after execution should return the active frame's logical view (already correct via oc.read_register("aN") with the running WB), but doing it through an explicit helper documents intent and shields the call sites from future WB drift.

  • [ ] Step 1: Write the failing test (windowed-reg setup with non-zero WB)

Append to crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs at the bottom of the H7 windowing section (after movsp_safe_path at line 1147):

// ── H7.9: Windowed-register setup at non-default WB (Plan 1.5 fix #2) ────────

/// Verify that `init_windowbase` + register setup work correctly when WB != 0.
///
/// Setup: WB=4, then write a3=0xCAFE_F00D.  After execution (just BREAK), the
/// test reads a3 back — it must equal what was written.  The Plan-1 runner
/// applied register writes BEFORE setting WB, so a3 went to the wrong physical
/// slot and the readback returned 0.
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn windowed_reg_setup_at_wb4_oracle() -> OracleCase {
    OracleCase::from_bytes(vec![])
        .setup(|st| {
            st.write_windowbase(4);
            st.write_windowstart(0x0010); // bit 4 set: frame at WB=4 is live
            st.write_reg("a3", 0xCAFE_F00D);
        })
        .expect(|st| {
            st.assert_reg("a3", 0xCAFE_F00D);
            st.assert_windowbase(4);
        })
}
  • [ ] Step 2: Run on sim (PASS) and HW (expect FAIL)
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle windowed_reg_setup_at_wb4_oracle_sim

Expected: PASS.

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  windowed_reg_setup_at_wb4_oracle_hw -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: FAIL with register a3: expected 0xCAFEF00D, got 0x00000000.

  • [ ] Step 3: Investigate OpenOCD physical-AR access

Probe whether the live board exposes physical AR registers as ar0..ar63:

killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -c "set ESP32_S3_ONLYCPU 1" \
  -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg ar0" -c "reg ar15" -c "reg ar63" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -15

Expected: either three hex values (physical AR access works directly) OR an error per line. Record the outcome:

  • If ar0..ar63 are accessible directly: implement write_logical_ar as a single dispatch to reg ar<phys>.

  • If not: implement write_logical_ar by temporarily rotating WB so the desired logical idx maps to a0..a3, writing a0..a3, then restoring WB.

  • [ ] Step 4a: Implement the helpers using direct physical AR access (preferred path)

If Step 3 showed ar0..ar63 work directly, append the following methods to OpenOcd in crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs (just below the SR dispatch added in Task 1):

    // ── Windowed-register translation (Plan 1.5 fix #2) ───────────────────────

    /// Read logical AR `idx` (0..=15) through the current `WindowBase`.
    ///
    /// Computes `phys = ((wb << 2) + idx) & 0x3F` and reads physical AR.
    pub fn read_logical_ar(&mut self, idx: u8) -> Result<u32> {
        assert!(idx < 16, "read_logical_ar: idx {idx} out of range 0..=15");
        let wb = self.read_register("windowbase")?;
        let phys = (((wb << 2) + idx as u32) & 0x3F) as u8;
        self.read_register(&format!("ar{phys}"))
    }

    /// Write `val` to logical AR `idx` (0..=15) through the current `WindowBase`.
    pub fn write_logical_ar(&mut self, idx: u8, val: u32) -> Result<()> {
        assert!(idx < 16, "write_logical_ar: idx {idx} out of range 0..=15");
        let wb = self.read_register("windowbase")?;
        let phys = (((wb << 2) + idx as u32) & 0x3F) as u8;
        self.write_register(&format!("ar{phys}"), val)
    }
  • [ ] Step 4b: Implement the helpers using WB rotation (fallback path)

If Step 3 showed ar0..ar63 are NOT directly accessible, use this version instead:

    // ── Windowed-register translation (Plan 1.5 fix #2) ───────────────────────

    /// Read logical AR `idx` (0..=15) through the current `WindowBase`.
    ///
    /// Temporarily rotates WB so that the desired physical slot maps to a0..a3,
    /// reads, then restores the original WB.
    pub fn read_logical_ar(&mut self, idx: u8) -> Result<u32> {
        assert!(idx < 16, "read_logical_ar: idx {idx} out of range 0..=15");
        let wb_orig = self.read_register("windowbase")?;
        let phys = ((wb_orig << 2) + idx as u32) & 0x3F;
        let wb_temp = phys >> 2;
        let logical_in_temp = (phys & 0x3) as u8;
        self.write_register("windowbase", wb_temp)?;
        let val = self.read_register(&format!("a{logical_in_temp}"))?;
        self.write_register("windowbase", wb_orig)?;
        Ok(val)
    }

    /// Write `val` to logical AR `idx` (0..=15) through the current `WindowBase`.
    pub fn write_logical_ar(&mut self, idx: u8, val: u32) -> Result<()> {
        assert!(idx < 16, "write_logical_ar: idx {idx} out of range 0..=15");
        let wb_orig = self.read_register("windowbase")?;
        let phys = ((wb_orig << 2) + idx as u32) & 0x3F;
        let wb_temp = phys >> 2;
        let logical_in_temp = (phys & 0x3) as u8;
        self.write_register("windowbase", wb_temp)?;
        self.write_register(&format!("a{logical_in_temp}"), val)?;
        self.write_register("windowbase", wb_orig)?;
        Ok(())
    }
  • [ ] Step 5: Reorder operations in capture_hw_state

In crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs, the current order in capture_hw_state is:

  1. (lines 921-922) Force WB=0, WS=1.
  2. (lines 925-933) Zero a0..a15 + SAR + SCOMPARE1.
  3. (lines 953-994) Apply test setup: write registers (line 965), write SRs, set WB/WS, set PS, write memory.

Move the WB/WS override BEFORE the register writes. Replace lines 960-994 with:

    // ── Apply test setup: WB/WS first, then registers, then SRs/PS/memory ──
    //
    // WB must be set BEFORE register writes so that st.write_reg("a3", ...)
    // routes to the correct physical AR slot.  Plan 1.5 fix #2.
    let mut init_state = OracleState::default();
    (case.setup)(&mut init_state);

    // Apply WindowBase / WindowStart overrides FIRST (before register writes).
    if let Some(wb) = init_state.init_windowbase {
        let _ = oc.write_register("windowbase", wb as u32);
    }
    if let Some(ws) = init_state.init_windowstart {
        let _ = oc.write_register("windowstart", ws as u32);
    }

    // Now write the test's logical-AR setup through the current WB.
    for (name, &val) in &init_state.regs {
        if let Some(idx) = parse_ar_name(name) {
            oc.write_logical_ar(idx, val)
                .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: write_logical_ar({name}={idx}) failed: {e}"));
        } else {
            // Non-AR names (PC, etc.) bypass the windowed translation.
            oc.write_register(name, val)
                .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: write_register({name}) failed: {e}"));
        }
    }

    // Apply SR setup (VECBASE, EPC1, INTENABLE, …) using the SR dispatch.
    for (&sr_id, &val) in &init_state.sr {
        let sr_name = sr_id_to_openocd_name(sr_id);
        if let Some(name) = sr_name {
            oc.write_sr(name, val)
                .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: write_sr({name}, 0x{val:08X}) failed: {e}"));
        }
    }

    // Apply PS overrides.
    if init_state.init_ps_excm.is_some() || init_state.init_ps_intlevel.is_some() {
        let mut ps = oc.read_register("ps").unwrap_or(0);
        if let Some(excm) = init_state.init_ps_excm {
            if excm { ps |= 1 << 4; } else { ps &= !(1 << 4); }
        }
        if let Some(level) = init_state.init_ps_intlevel {
            ps = (ps & !0xF) | (level as u32 & 0xF);
        }
        let _ = oc.write_register("ps", ps);
    }

    // Write setup memory into HW via OpenOCD.
    for (&addr, &val) in &init_state.mem {
        oc.write_memory(addr, &[val])
            .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: write_memory(0x{addr:08X}) failed: {e}"));
    }

(Importantly: parse_ar_name is already defined at crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs:530. This task uses it from the existing definition; no new helper needed.)

  • [ ] Step 6: Update end-state register capture to use logical AR helpers

Replace the AR capture loop in capture_hw_state (around lines 1015-1020):

    for i in 0u32..16 {
        let name = format!("a{}", i);
        let val = oc.read_register(&name)
            .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: read_register({name}) failed: {e}"));
        end.regs.insert(name, val);
    }

with:

    for i in 0u8..16 {
        let val = oc.read_logical_ar(i)
            .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: read_logical_ar({i}) failed: {e}"));
        end.regs.insert(format!("a{}", i), val);
    }
  • [ ] Step 7: Run the failing test on HW; expect PASS
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  windowed_reg_setup_at_wb4_oracle -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: both windowed_reg_setup_at_wb4_oracle_hw and windowed_reg_setup_at_wb4_oracle_diff PASS.

  • [ ] Step 8: Run sim suite; expect ≥ 463 passing
cargo test --workspace \
  --exclude firmware \
  --exclude firmware-ci-fixture \
  --exclude riscv-ci-fixture \
  2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: ≥ 463 (baseline + 2 new sim tests so far).

  • [ ] Step 9: Run full HW oracle bank
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: a noticeable jump in passing variants — typically 8-10 tests in the windowing/exception banks were blocked by this; they should now PASS. Record new total.

  • [ ] Step 10: Commit
git add crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs
git commit -m "fix(hw-oracle): windowed-register translation through current WB

Plan 1.5 fix #2: tests that pre-load WindowBase to a non-default value
saw their setup register writes go to the wrong physical AR slot
because capture_hw_state wrote registers BEFORE setting WB. Adds
read_logical_ar/write_logical_ar that compute phys = ((wb<<2)+idx)&0x3F
and reorders capture_hw_state to set WB first. Adds
windowed_reg_setup_at_wb4_oracle as a regression."
git push

docs.title: Task 3: VECBASE write sequencer (HW root cause #3)
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs (add set_vecbase with halt-write-flush sequence) - Modify: crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs:1080-1111 (use set_vecbase for VECBASE setup)

Background: Tests like vecbase_relocation_oracle_hw, interrupt_dispatch_oracle_hw, and entry_window_overflow_of4_hw set VECBASE in setup so the exception/interrupt vector points into the oracle IRAM where a BREAK is planted. On the live board, oc.write_register("vecbase", IRAM_BASE) does write the SR but the next exception still vectors to the ROM address (0x40000300), suggesting the VECBASE write doesn't take effect in the CPU's exception-dispatch path until something flushes pipeline state.

This task probes for the working sequence and wraps it in set_vecbase.

  • [ ] Step 1: Probe the failing case on the live board
killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -c "set ESP32_S3_ONLYCPU 1" \
  -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg vecbase" \
  -c "reg vecbase 0x40370000" \
  -c "reg vecbase" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -15

Expected: the second reg vecbase shows 0x40370000. If it does, the SR write itself is correct — the problem is that the next exception still vectors to the ROM address.

  • [ ] Step 2: Probe whether xtensa wsr vecbase differs
killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -c "set ESP32_S3_ONLYCPU 1" \
  -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "xtensa wsr vecbase 0x40370000" \
  -c "xtensa rsr vecbase" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -15

Expected: 0x40370000. If this works AND the previous form works, the SR write itself is fine. The problem is downstream.

  • [ ] Step 3: Probe with an explicit arm semihosting_command or pipeline flush

OpenOCD's Xtensa target supports an explicit pipeline flush via xtensa cpu invalidate or xtensa cpu_disable_invalidate; for ESP32-S3 the safest is a step after the write (the single-step forces the CPU to re-fetch using the new SR state):

killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -c "set ESP32_S3_ONLYCPU 1" \
  -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg vecbase 0x40370000" \
  -c "reg vecbase" \
  -c "step" \
  -c "halt" \
  -c "reg vecbase" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -15

Note the value of vecbase before and after step + halt. Record findings.

  • [ ] Step 4: Add set_vecbase to openocd.rs

Insert just below the windowed-AR helpers added in Task 2:

    // ── VECBASE write sequencer (Plan 1.5 fix #3) ─────────────────────────────

    /// Write VECBASE and ensure the CPU's exception dispatch picks up the new
    /// value before the next resume.
    ///
    /// On the ESP32-S3 LX7 target, a plain `reg vecbase <addr>` writes the SR
    /// but the next exception may still vector to the previous VECBASE because
    /// the pipeline hasn't re-fetched from the new SR.  This sequence ensures
    /// the write is observed by the exception dispatch path:
    ///
    ///   halt → write SR → halt (synchronises OpenOCD target state).
    ///
    /// If empirical investigation in Task 3 reveals additional steps are
    /// required (e.g. an explicit pipeline flush or a single-step), they are
    /// added here.
    pub fn set_vecbase(&mut self, addr: u32) -> Result<()> {
        // Ensure the target is halted so the SR write is well-defined.
        let _ = self.tcl("halt");
        // Write VECBASE through the SR dispatch (form chosen per-session).
        self.write_sr("vecbase", addr)?;
        // Synchronise OpenOCD's idea of target state.
        let _ = self.tcl("halt");
        Ok(())
    }

If Step 3 indicated that an additional step is needed before the change takes effect, add it after write_sr but BEFORE the second halt. The implementer must verify by running the failing test (Step 6) and adjusting if needed.

  • [ ] Step 5: Route VECBASE writes through set_vecbase in capture_hw_state

In crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs, replace the SR setup loop modified in Task 2 with a special-case for VECBASE:

    // Apply SR setup (VECBASE, EPC1, INTENABLE, …) using the SR dispatch.
    // VECBASE goes through set_vecbase to ensure the exception dispatch picks
    // up the new value before the next resume (Plan 1.5 fix #3).
    use labwired_core::cpu::xtensa_sr::VECBASE;
    for (&sr_id, &val) in &init_state.sr {
        if sr_id == VECBASE {
            oc.set_vecbase(val)
                .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: set_vecbase(0x{val:08X}) failed: {e}"));
        } else if let Some(name) = sr_id_to_openocd_name(sr_id) {
            oc.write_sr(name, val)
                .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: write_sr({name}, 0x{val:08X}) failed: {e}"));
        }
    }
  • [ ] Step 6: Run the previously-failing VECBASE-dependent tests
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  vecbase_relocation_oracle_hw \
  interrupt_dispatch_oracle_hw \
  entry_window_overflow_of4_hw \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: at least vecbase_relocation_oracle_hw PASSes. The other two depend on additional fixes; if either still fails, that's expected and will be addressed by Task 4 (illegal opcode replacements) or Task 5 (sub-word DRAM) downstream.

  • [ ] Step 7: Run sim + HW bank
cargo test --workspace \
  --exclude firmware --exclude firmware-ci-fixture --exclude riscv-ci-fixture \
  2>&1 | tail -5

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: sim baseline holds; HW total ≥ previous + at least 1 (vecbase_relocation_oracle_hw + diff).

  • [ ] Step 8: Commit
git add crates/hw-oracle/src/openocd.rs crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs
git commit -m "fix(hw-oracle): VECBASE write sequencer

Plan 1.5 fix #3: VECBASE writes through plain 'reg vecbase' did not
take effect for the next exception dispatch on the live ESP32-S3.
New set_vecbase wraps a halt → write_sr → halt sequence and replaces
the direct write_register call in capture_hw_state."
git push

docs.title: Task 4: Replace wrong-illegal-opcode tests (HW root cause #4)
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs:1180-1224 (illegal_instruction_oracle)

Background: The existing illegal_instruction_oracle uses opcode 0x008530 ("decode: op0=0 (QRST), op1=0, op2=0 (ST0), r=8 → Unknown"). On the live LX7, 0x008530 does NOT raise IllegalInstruction — silicon decodes it as a legal-but-undocumented instruction the plan author missed. We replace this with an opcode that xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as rejects AND that the live board confirms raises EXCCAUSE=0.

  • [ ] Step 1: Generate candidate truly-illegal opcodes via the assembler

The assembler rejects truly-invalid opcodes. Probe a few candidate bit patterns:

mkdir -p /tmp/illegal_probe && cd /tmp/illegal_probe

# Candidate 1: all-1s 24-bit pattern.
echo '.word 0xFFFFFF' | xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as -o c1.o - 2>&1 || true

# Candidate 2: a reserved op0=0xF, op1=0xF, op2=0xF combination.
echo '.word 0xFFF0FF' | xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as -o c2.o - 2>&1 || true

# Candidate 3: 0x00FFFF (op0=F is reserved on LX7).
echo '.word 0x00FFFF' | xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as -o c3.o - 2>&1 || true

For each candidate, the assembler's behavior is informative — .word always succeeds (it just emits raw bytes), so the probe instead is whether objdump decodes it as a real instruction:

for c in c1 c2 c3; do
  if [ -f "$c.o" ]; then
    echo "=== $c ==="
    xtensa-esp32s3-elf-objdump -d "$c.o" 2>&1 | head -10
  fi
done

Expected: at least one decodes as a .byte sequence or shows Illegal instruction / no decode. Pick the first candidate that objdump does NOT recognize as a legal instruction. Record the chosen 24-bit value.

If all three candidates decode as legal instructions, try 0x000000, 0x008550, 0x008580, 0x008590, 0x008500 (other ST0 r values besides r=8) until one is rejected by objdump.

  • [ ] Step 2: Probe the chosen candidate on the live board

Write a tiny program with the chosen opcode + BREAK and run it through the existing harness in a one-shot ad-hoc test. Use the chosen opcode (here we assume 0x000000 was picked; substitute the actual chosen value):

killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -c "set ESP32_S3_ONLYCPU 1" \
  -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg vecbase 0x40370000" \
  -c "mww 0x40370000 0x000000" \
  -c "mww 0x40370300 0x0041F0" \
  -c "reg pc 0x40370000" \
  -c "resume" \
  -c "sleep 200" \
  -c "halt" \
  -c "reg exccause" \
  -c "reg epc1" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -15

Expected: exccause is 0x00000000 (IllegalInstructionCause = 0) and epc1 is 0x40370000. Record outcome.

If the chosen opcode does NOT raise EXCCAUSE=0, try the next candidate from Step 1 until one does.

  • [ ] Step 3: Update illegal_instruction_oracle to use the verified opcode

Edit crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs lines 1180-1224. Replace the test body with the new opcode (substitute the verified value from Step 2 — example uses 0x000000):

// ── H8.1: Illegal instruction raises EXCCAUSE=0 ──────────────────────────────

/// Execute a truly-illegal opcode and verify IllegalInstruction exception entry.
///
/// Opcode `0x000000` is rejected by xtensa-esp32s3-elf-objdump and confirmed
/// to raise EXCCAUSE=0 on the live ESP32-S3-Zero.  (The previous opcode
/// 0x008530 was decoded as a legal-but-undocumented instruction by silicon —
/// Plan 1.5 fix #4.)
///
/// Program:
///   IRAM_BASE+0x000: [0x00, 0x00, 0x00]  — illegal opcode
///   IRAM_BASE+0x300: BREAK  ← exception kernel vector landing pad
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn illegal_instruction_oracle() -> OracleCase {
    let mut prog = vec![0u8; 0x303];
    prog[0..3].copy_from_slice(&[0x00, 0x00, 0x00]); // illegal opcode (verified)
    prog[0x300..0x303].copy_from_slice(&[0xF0, 0x41, 0x00]); // BREAK at exception vector
    OracleCase::from_bytes(prog)
        .setup(|st| {
            st.write_vecbase(IRAM_BASE);
        })
        .expect(|st| {
            st.assert_pc(IRAM_BASE + 0x300);
            st.assert_epc1(IRAM_BASE);
            st.assert_exccause(0);
            st.assert_excm(true);
        })
}
  • [ ] Step 4: Verify on sim and HW
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle illegal_instruction_oracle_sim
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  illegal_instruction_oracle -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: all three variants (_sim, _hw, _diff) PASS.

  • [ ] Step 5: Confirm the simulator decoder treats the new opcode as Unknown

The replacement opcode must decode to Unknown in the simulator so that the existing Unknown(_) => return self.raise_general_exception(0) path at crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs:1208 fires. Confirm via:

cargo test -p labwired-core --lib decoder::xtensa 2>&1 | tail -10

Expected: PASS. If the new opcode happens to decode as a legal instruction in the sim decoder, fix the decoder to treat it as Unknown — but this is unlikely given the assembler also rejects it.

  • [ ] Step 6: Run sim + HW bank
cargo test --workspace \
  --exclude firmware --exclude firmware-ci-fixture --exclude riscv-ci-fixture \
  2>&1 | tail -5

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: sim count unchanged (test was modified, not added); HW count up by at least 1.

  • [ ] Step 7: Commit
git add crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs
git commit -m "fix(hw-oracle): replace illegal_instruction_oracle opcode

Plan 1.5 fix #4: the previous 0x008530 opcode is decoded as a
legal-but-undocumented instruction by ESP32-S3 silicon. Replaced with
<chosen opcode>, verified by objdump rejection AND live-board EXCCAUSE=0
readback. Sim Unknown→IllegalInstruction path is unchanged."
git push

(Substitute <chosen opcode> in the commit message.)

docs.title: Task 5: Sub-word DRAM readback workaround (HW root cause #5)
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs — the affected sub-word load tests at l8ui_oracle (line 398), l16ui_oracle (424), l16si_oracle (449), and the corresponding store tests s8i_oracle (497), s16i_oracle (522) - Modify: docs/hw-oracle-runner-setup.md — document the workaround

Background: Sub-word load tests (L8UI, L16UI, L16SI) execute correctly on the live board — the load does update the destination AR — but reading that AR back via OpenOCD returns 0 in some configurations. Suspected cause: OpenOCD's register cache is not invalidated between the executed step and the subsequent reg aN read. Two practical fixes:

  • (A) Workaround in the fixture program: append a no-op move (OR aT, aT, a0) after the load. The OR re-writes the AR through a normal AR write path which OpenOCD's register cache observes correctly.
  • (B) Force OpenOCD register cache invalidation between resume and read: OpenOCD supports reg <name> force or flush_count operations. The implementer should probe this on the board.

The plan uses (A) as the primary fix because it's local to the test fixtures and works regardless of OpenOCD version. (B) is an investigation thread to keep open in docs/hw-oracle-runner-setup.md.

  • [ ] Step 1: Probe the failing case to confirm root cause

Write a tiny fixture: load 0xAB from DRAM into a3, then BREAK. Run on HW and read a3:

killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -c "set ESP32_S3_ONLYCPU 1" \
  -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "mww 0x3FC89000 0x000000AB" \
  -c "mww 0x40370000 0xFF8302" \
  -c "mwb 0x40370003 0xF0" -c "mwb 0x40370004 0x41" -c "mwb 0x40370005 0x00" \
  -c "reg a4 0x3FC89000" \
  -c "reg pc 0x40370000" \
  -c "resume" -c "sleep 200" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg a3" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -15

(0xFF8302 is L8UI a3, a4, 0xFF per existing decoder convention; check the decoder for the exact encoding if the load doesn't fire.)

Expected: a3 reads 0x000000AB. If it reads 0x00000000, the bug is reproduced and the workaround in Step 2 will fix it.

  • [ ] Step 2: Probe the OR-workaround fix

Append OR a3, a3, a0 (0x230020) after the load, BEFORE the BREAK:

killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -c "set ESP32_S3_ONLYCPU 1" \
  -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "mww 0x3FC89000 0x000000AB" \
  -c "mwb 0x40370000 0x82" -c "mwb 0x40370001 0x34" -c "mwb 0x40370002 0xFF" \
  -c "mwb 0x40370003 0x20" -c "mwb 0x40370004 0x00" -c "mwb 0x40370005 0x23" \
  -c "mwb 0x40370006 0xF0" -c "mwb 0x40370007 0x41" -c "mwb 0x40370008 0x00" \
  -c "reg a4 0x3FC89000" \
  -c "reg pc 0x40370000" \
  -c "resume" -c "sleep 200" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg a3" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -15

Expected: a3 now reads 0x000000AB. If yes, (A) is the fix. If no, fall back to investigating (B) before continuing.

  • [ ] Step 3: Inspect the affected sub-word load oracle tests
grep -A 25 "fn l8ui_oracle\|fn l16ui_oracle\|fn l16si_oracle" \
  crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs | head -90

For each test, identify the encoding bytes used and the destination AR (at). The fix is to append OR aAt, aAt, a0 (3-byte instruction, encoded as (0x2 << 20) | (3 << 16) | (At << 12) | (At << 8) | (0 << 4) | 0 = 0x23AT_A0_00 LE). Compute the bytes for each at:

For at = 3: w = 0x230030, bytes [0x30, 0x00, 0x23]. Wait — the OR encoding is RRR with op0=0, op1=0, op2=2: w = (At << 12) | (As << 8) | (At << 4) | (op2 << 16) | … actually the simplest verification:

mkdir -p /tmp/or_probe && cd /tmp/or_probe
echo 'or a3, a3, a0' | xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as -o or.o -
xtensa-esp32s3-elf-objdump -d or.o

Use the bytes objdump prints. Repeat for each at value used by l8ui_oracle / l16ui_oracle / l16si_oracle.

  • [ ] Step 4: Update the affected sub-word load tests with the OR workaround

For each of l8ui_oracle, l16ui_oracle, l16si_oracle, modify the program bytes to insert the OR instruction between the load and the auto-appended BREAK. Example for l8ui_oracle (assuming at=3):

Look at the existing test:

sed -n '398,422p' crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs

Find the line that builds the program bytes (likely OracleCase::asm(".word 0x...") or OracleCase::from_bytes(vec![...])). Append the OR encoding bytes BEFORE the call to .setup() / .expect(). If the test uses OracleCase::asm, switch to OracleCase::from_bytes and concatenate the load bytes + OR bytes; the BREAK is auto-appended.

Add a comment above the OR insertion:

// Plan 1.5 fix #5: append OR aT, aT, a0 to reify the L8UI writeback
// through a normal AR write path, working around an OpenOCD register-cache
// staleness issue on the live ESP32-S3.
  • [ ] Step 5: Verify the modified tests pass on sim and HW
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle l8ui_oracle_sim l16ui_oracle_sim l16si_oracle_sim
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  l8ui_oracle l16ui_oracle l16si_oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: all _sim, _hw, _diff variants PASS.

  • [ ] Step 6: Investigate sub-word stores (S8I, S16I)

The store tests verify by reading memory back, not a register, so they may not need the workaround. Run them as-is to confirm:

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  s8i_oracle s16i_oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1

If they pass, no fix needed for stores. If they fail, investigate separately — likely a different root cause (DRAM alias write didn't reach SRAM) and the workaround for stores is to have the test program load from the same address into a temp register and verify that, OR to add an explicit dsync instruction (0x00207F → dsync) after the store.

  • [ ] Step 7: Document the workaround in the runner-setup doc

Append to docs/hw-oracle-runner-setup.md:

## Sub-word load readback workaround (Plan 1.5)

Sub-word load instructions (`L8UI`, `L16UI`, `L16SI`) on the live ESP32-S3-Zero
correctly update the destination AR, but reading that AR back via OpenOCD
returns 0 in some configurations.  Root cause is suspected to be OpenOCD's
register cache not being invalidated between the executed step and the
subsequent `reg aN` read.

**Workaround (current):** oracle tests exercising sub-word loads append an
`OR aT, aT, a0` after the load.  The OR re-writes the AR through a normal
AR write path which OpenOCD's register cache observes correctly.

**Future investigation:** if a future OpenOCD version supports an explicit
register-cache invalidation command (e.g. `reg aN force` or
`xtensa cpu_invalidate_caches`), the workaround can be removed and the
runner can issue the invalidation directly between resume and capture.
  • [ ] Step 8: Run the full HW oracle bank
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tail -10

Expected: HW count up by 6+ (the three load tests × 2 variants each = 6 minimum, plus any cascade from earlier fixes).

  • [ ] Step 9: Commit
git add crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs docs/hw-oracle-runner-setup.md
git commit -m "fix(hw-oracle): sub-word load readback via OR-aT-aT-a0 workaround

Plan 1.5 fix #5: L8UI/L16UI/L16SI on the live ESP32-S3 update the
destination AR correctly but OpenOCD's register cache returns 0 on
readback. Append OR aT, aT, a0 after each sub-word load to reify the
writeback through a normal AR write path. Documented in
hw-oracle-runner-setup.md."
git push

docs.title: Task 6: ROTW privilege check
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs:1067 (Rotw { n } arm) - Test: crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs (new oracle test)

Background: ROTW is a privileged instruction. When PS.RING != 0 it raises PrivilegedCause (EXCCAUSE=8). Plan 1 deferred the check; this closes that gap. The test needs a write_ps_ring helper that doesn't exist yet, so we add the helper first, then the test, then the fix.

  • [ ] Step 1: Add the init_ps_ring field to OracleState

In crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs, add to the OracleState struct (around line 110, near other init_ps_* fields):

    /// If `Some(ring)`, override `PS.RING` before execution.
    pub init_ps_ring: Option<u8>,
  • [ ] Step 2: Add the write_ps_ring setter near write_ps_excm

In the same file around line 210, add:

    /// Override `PS.RING` (bits 7:6) before execution.
    pub fn write_ps_ring(&mut self, ring: u8) {
        assert!(ring <= 3, "write_ps_ring: ring {ring} out of range 0..=3");
        self.init_ps_ring = Some(ring);
    }
  • [ ] Step 3: Apply init_ps_ring in both capture_sim_state and capture_hw_state

In capture_sim_state (around line 707, near the other PS overrides):

    // Apply PS.RING override.
    if let Some(ring) = init_state.init_ps_ring {
        cpu.ps.set_ring(ring);
    }

In capture_hw_state, find the existing PS read-modify-write block (around lines 985-994 — already modified by Task 6 will not have applied yet, so look for if init_state.init_ps_excm.is_some() || init_state.init_ps_intlevel.is_some()) and extend it:

    if init_state.init_ps_excm.is_some()
        || init_state.init_ps_intlevel.is_some()
        || init_state.init_ps_ring.is_some()
    {
        let mut ps = oc.read_register("ps").unwrap_or(0);
        if let Some(excm) = init_state.init_ps_excm {
            if excm { ps |= 1 << 4; } else { ps &= !(1 << 4); }
        }
        if let Some(level) = init_state.init_ps_intlevel {
            ps = (ps & !0xF) | (level as u32 & 0xF);
        }
        if let Some(ring) = init_state.init_ps_ring {
            ps = (ps & !(0x3 << 6)) | ((ring as u32 & 0x3) << 6);
        }
        let _ = oc.write_register("ps", ps);
    }
  • [ ] Step 4: Write the failing test

Append to crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs after rotw_1 (line 1119):

// ── H7.10: ROTW raises PrivilegedCause when PS.RING != 0 (Plan 1.5) ──────────

/// ROTW from a non-privileged ring (PS.RING != 0) must raise
/// PrivilegedCause (EXCCAUSE=8) and vector to VECBASE+0x300, NOT rotate WB.
///
/// Plan 1 deferred this check; Plan 1.5 closes the gap.
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn rotw_privileged_at_ring1() -> OracleCase {
    // ROTW 1: w=0x408010, bytes [0x10, 0x80, 0x40].
    let mut prog = vec![0u8; 0x303];
    prog[0..3].copy_from_slice(&[0x10, 0x80, 0x40]);
    prog[0x300..0x303].copy_from_slice(&[0xF0, 0x41, 0x00]); // BREAK at exception vector
    OracleCase::from_bytes(prog)
        .setup(|st| {
            st.write_vecbase(IRAM_BASE);
            st.write_ps_ring(1);
        })
        .expect(|st| {
            // Vectored to kernel exception handler (VECBASE + 0x300).
            st.assert_pc(IRAM_BASE + 0x300);
            // EPC1 holds the ROTW instruction address.
            st.assert_epc1(IRAM_BASE);
            // EXCCAUSE = 8: PrivilegedCause.
            st.assert_exccause(8);
        })
}
  • [ ] Step 5: Run the test on sim; expect FAIL (ROTW currently doesn't check ring)
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle rotw_privileged_at_ring1_sim

Expected: FAIL with EXCCAUSE: expected 8, got 0 (or similar) because the current ROTW implementation does not check ring.

  • [ ] Step 6: Add the privilege check to ROTW

In crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs, modify the Rotw { n } arm (line 1067). Replace:

            Rotw { n } => {
                let wb = self.regs.windowbase();
                // n is i8 (range -8..=+7); wrapping add modulo 16.
                let wb_new = (wb as i32).wrapping_add(n as i32).rem_euclid(16) as u8;
                self.regs.set_windowbase(wb_new);
                // WindowStart is NOT modified (ISA RM §8 ROTW).
                self.pc = self.pc.wrapping_add(len);
            }

with:

            // ROTW n: rotate WindowBase by n (4-bit signed, range -8..=+7).
            //
            // ISA RM §8 ROTW semantics:
            //   if PS.RING != 0: raise PrivilegedCause (EXCCAUSE=8)
            //   else: WindowBase = (WindowBase + n) mod 16, WindowStart unchanged.
            //
            // Plan 1.5 closure: the privilege check is now enforced.
            Rotw { n } => {
                if self.ps.ring() != 0 {
                    return self.raise_general_exception(8);
                }
                let wb = self.regs.windowbase();
                let wb_new = (wb as i32).wrapping_add(n as i32).rem_euclid(16) as u8;
                self.regs.set_windowbase(wb_new);
                self.pc = self.pc.wrapping_add(len);
            }

Also delete the TODO(plan-priv): line above the original arm (lines 1065-1066) since it's now closed.

  • [ ] Step 7: Run the test on sim; expect PASS
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle rotw_privileged_at_ring1_sim

Expected: PASS.

  • [ ] Step 8: Run the test on HW
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  rotw_privileged_at_ring1 -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: PASS for _hw and _diff. If _hw fails, investigate whether the live board's PS.RING write is being respected (it should be at this point given the SR dispatch and reorder fixes from Tasks 1-3).

  • [ ] Step 9: Confirm existing ROTW tests still pass
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle rotw_1
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  rotw_1 -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: all variants PASS (existing test runs at default ring 0).

  • [ ] Step 10: Run sim + HW bank
cargo test --workspace \
  --exclude firmware --exclude firmware-ci-fixture --exclude riscv-ci-fixture \
  2>&1 | tail -5

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: sim count + 1; HW count + 2 (or + 1 if _hw fails as a known limitation).

  • [ ] Step 11: Update the case study's "Known Gaps" entry

Edit docs/case_study_esp32s3_plan1.md and remove the bullet:

**ROTW privilege check.** `ROTW` does not enforce `PS.RING=0`. Plan 1 does not model privilege rings; this is a known delta from real silicon behavior.

(This is a partial-cleanup step; full case study update is Task 10.)

  • [ ] Step 12: Commit
git add crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs docs/case_study_esp32s3_plan1.md
git commit -m "fix(xtensa): ROTW privilege check (PS.RING != 0 → PrivilegedCause)

Plan 1.5: ROTW now raises PrivilegedCause (EXCCAUSE=8) when PS.RING
is non-zero, matching ISA RM §8. Adds rotw_privileged_at_ring1 oracle
test, OracleState::write_ps_ring helper, and removes the corresponding
Known Gap from the Plan 1 case study."
git push

docs.title: Task 7: S32E IllegalInstruction outside vector context
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs:1398-1420 (S32E/L32E disambiguation in step()) - Test: crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs (new oracle test)

Background: When PS.EXCM == 0 and the next 3 bytes form an S32E (subop=4, op0=9, byte2 low nibble=0) pattern, the current code falls through and decodes as a 2-byte narrow S32I.N. Real silicon raises IllegalInstruction in some scenarios. Per the spec, we empirically verify on HW first to determine the correct behavior, then align the simulator.

  • [ ] Step 1: Probe what real silicon does for S32E with PS.EXCM=0

S32E a3, a4, -4 encodes as: subop=4 (S32E), op0=9, t=at=3, s=as_=4, imm=-4 (offset). Per the existing comment, byte0 low nibble = 0x9, byte0 high nibble = 0x4 → byte0 = 0x49. We need a 3-byte sequence.

Use the assembler to generate the canonical encoding:

mkdir -p /tmp/s32e_probe && cd /tmp/s32e_probe
echo 's32e a3, a4, -4' | xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as -o s.o -
xtensa-esp32s3-elf-objdump -d s.o

Record the 3 bytes from objdump.

Run on HW with PS.EXCM=0 (the runner sets this by default after clean_ps write at lib.rs line 953):

killall openocd 2>/dev/null
openocd -c "set ESP32_S3_ONLYCPU 1" \
  -f interface/esp_usb_jtag.cfg -f target/esp32s3.cfg \
  -c "init" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg vecbase 0x40370000" \
  -c "mwb 0x40370000 <S32E_byte0>" -c "mwb 0x40370001 <S32E_byte1>" -c "mwb 0x40370002 <S32E_byte2>" \
  -c "mwb 0x40370300 0xF0" -c "mwb 0x40370301 0x41" -c "mwb 0x40370302 0x00" \
  -c "reg ps 0x00040000" \
  -c "reg pc 0x40370000" \
  -c "resume" -c "sleep 200" -c "halt" \
  -c "reg pc" -c "reg exccause" -c "reg epc1" \
  -c "shutdown" 2>&1 | tail -15

(Substitute <S32E_byte0> etc. with the bytes from the assembler.)

Expected: one of two outcomes: - pc = 0x40370300, exccause = 0x00000000, epc1 = 0x40370000 → silicon raises IllegalInstruction → align sim by raising IllegalInstruction in the disambiguation when EXCM=0. - pc != 0x40370300 → silicon executes something (likely treats as narrow S32I.N) → existing sim behavior is correct; close the Known Gap as "by design — matches silicon".

Record the outcome.

  • [ ] Step 2: Decision branch based on probe outcome

If silicon raises IllegalInstruction, proceed to Step 3 (implement the fix). If silicon doesn't, skip to Step 8 (just close the Known Gap).

  • [ ] Step 3 (only if silicon raises IllegalInstruction): Write the failing test

Append to crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs after s32e_inside_vector (around line 1107):

// ── H7.11: S32E outside vector context raises IllegalInstruction (Plan 1.5) ──

/// S32E with PS.EXCM=0 raises IllegalInstruction on silicon (verified on the
/// live ESP32-S3-Zero). The Plan-1 simulator silently fell through to the
/// narrow S32I.N decode; Plan 1.5 closes this digital-twin gap.
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn s32e_outside_vector_illegal() -> OracleCase {
    // S32E a3, a4, -4: bytes from the assembler probe (substitute actual values).
    // Replace the three placeholder bytes below with the verified S32E encoding.
    let s32e_bytes = [/* byte0 */ 0x49, /* byte1 */ 0x43, /* byte2 */ 0xF0];
    let mut prog = vec![0u8; 0x303];
    prog[0..3].copy_from_slice(&s32e_bytes);
    prog[0x300..0x303].copy_from_slice(&[0xF0, 0x41, 0x00]); // BREAK at vector
    OracleCase::from_bytes(prog)
        .setup(|st| {
            st.write_vecbase(IRAM_BASE);
            // PS.EXCM=0 explicitly (default after clean_ps in capture_hw_state).
            st.write_ps_excm(false);
            st.write_reg("a4", IRAM_BASE + 0x1000); // dummy AS_ — never used
        })
        .expect(|st| {
            st.assert_pc(IRAM_BASE + 0x300);
            st.assert_epc1(IRAM_BASE);
            st.assert_exccause(0);
            st.assert_excm(true);
        })
}

Substitute the three bytes with the values from the assembler in Step 1.

  • [ ] Step 4 (only if proceeding from Step 3): Run the test on sim; expect FAIL
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle s32e_outside_vector_illegal_sim

Expected: FAIL — sim falls through to narrow S32I.N execution and never raises EXCCAUSE=0.

  • [ ] Step 5 (only if proceeding from Step 4): Implement the fix in step()

In crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs:1398-1420, modify the disambiguation:

        // Plan 1.5 fix: also detect S32E/L32E patterns when EXCM=0 and raise
        // IllegalInstruction (per HW probe — silicon raises EXCCAUSE=0 here).
        let is_s32e_or_l32e_excm0 = if !self.ps.excm() && len == 2 && (b0 & 0x0F) == 0x9 {
            let b2 = bus.read_u8(pc as u64 + 2)?;
            let subop = (b0 >> 4) & 0xF;
            (subop == 4 || subop == 0) && (b2 & 0x0F) == 0
        } else {
            false
        };

        if is_s32e_or_l32e_excm0 {
            // Outside an exception context: raise IllegalInstruction directly,
            // do not decode as wide and do not fall through to narrow S32I.N.
            return self.raise_general_exception(0);
        }

        let is_s32e_or_l32e = if self.ps.excm() && len == 2 && (b0 & 0x0F) == 0x9 {
            // (existing logic unchanged)
            let b2 = bus.read_u8(pc as u64 + 2)?;
            let subop = (b0 >> 4) & 0xF;
            (subop == 4 || subop == 0) && (b2 & 0x0F) == 0
        } else {
            false
        };
        // (rest unchanged)

Caution: the is_s32e_or_l32e_excm0 check is sensitive — it must NOT false-positive on legitimate S32I.N a0, a1, 0 followed by another instruction whose byte0 has low nibble 0. Re-read the existing comment block at lines 1378-1397 to understand the encoding overlap. If the probe in Step 1 showed the silicon distinguishes by checking that byte2 also matches the S32E/L32E format completely (e.g., op1=0 AND specific bits in [11:8] / [15:12]), tighten the check accordingly.

  • [ ] Step 6 (only if proceeding from Step 5): Verify
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle s32e_outside_vector_illegal_sim
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  s32e_outside_vector_illegal -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: all variants PASS.

Critical regression check — make sure normal narrow S32I.N still works:

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle s32i_oracle_sim
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle s32i_oracle -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: all variants PASS. If FAIL, the disambiguation false-positives — tighten the byte-pattern check.

  • [ ] Step 7 (only if proceeding from Step 6): Update Known Gaps

Edit docs/case_study_esp32s3_plan1.md and remove the bullet:

**S32E outside a vector context.** The `PS.EXCM` gate in `step()` correctly prevents S32E from being decoded in normal code, but if firmware somehow reaches an S32E at a non-vector address without EXCM set, the simulator silently falls through to `S32I.N` rather than raising `IllegalInstruction`. Real hardware raises an exception. This is a documented digital-twin gap.
  • [ ] Step 8 (only if Step 2 went the OTHER way — silicon does NOT raise IllegalInstruction): Update Known Gaps with the new ground truth

If the probe showed silicon executes the bytes as narrow S32I.N when EXCM=0, the existing simulator behavior IS correct. Edit docs/case_study_esp32s3_plan1.md and replace the bullet with:

**S32E outside a vector context — by design.** When `PS.EXCM=0` and the byte pattern matches S32E/L32E, silicon decodes as narrow `S32I.N` (verified on the live ESP32-S3-Zero in Plan 1.5). The simulator matches this behavior. This is no longer a digital-twin gap.
  • [ ] Step 9: Run sim + HW bank
cargo test --workspace \
  --exclude firmware --exclude firmware-ci-fixture --exclude riscv-ci-fixture \
  2>&1 | tail -5

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: sim count up by ≥ 1 (only if Step 3's test was added); HW count up by ≥ 2 (only if Step 3's test was added).

  • [ ] Step 10: Commit
git add crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs docs/case_study_esp32s3_plan1.md
git commit -m "fix(xtensa): S32E outside vector context — align with silicon

Plan 1.5: empirically determined silicon behavior for S32E with
PS.EXCM=0 (verified on live ESP32-S3-Zero). Either implements the
IllegalInstruction path (if silicon raises) or documents that the
existing fall-through-to-S32I.N is correct (if silicon decodes as
narrow). Updates Known Gaps in case study accordingly."
git push

(Adjust the commit message based on which branch was taken at Step 2.)

docs.title: Task 8: RFWU UF8/UF12 generalisation
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs:1192-1199 (Rfwu arm) - Test: crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs (new oracle tests for UF8 and UF12)

Background: Per the existing comment at lines 1182-1188, the RFWU implementation only correctly handles the UF4 case. The UF8 and UF12 cases require rotating WindowBase by N rather than 1. The fix generalises the rotation to use the underflow level encoded in the instruction.

The Xtensa ISA defines RFWU as a single instruction (no immediate variant) — the "underflow level" is implicit in which underflow vector handler invoked it. Looking at the actual encoding in the decoder will clarify whether RFWU encodes N as part of the opcode.

  • [ ] Step 1: Inspect the RFWU encoding and decoder
grep -n "Rfwu\|RFWU\|rfwu" crates/core/src/decoder/xtensa.rs | head -10
grep -n "Rfwu" crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs | head -10

Determine whether Rfwu is a single variant or whether the decoder emits e.g., Rfwu { level }.

  • [ ] Step 2 (Branch A — Rfwu is a single variant with no level field):

If the decoder emits a bare Rfwu with no level, the underflow level must be inferred from EPC1 (which points back to the RETW instruction whose execution triggered the underflow). The cleanest fix is to compute the level from CALLINC:

In crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs:1192-1199, replace:

            Rfwu => {
                let wb_old = self.regs.windowbase();
                let wb_new = wb_old.wrapping_sub(1) & 0x0F;
                self.regs.set_windowbase(wb_new);
                self.regs.set_windowstart_bit(wb_new, true);
                self.ps.set_excm(false);
                self.pc = self.sr.read(EPC1);
            }

with:

            // RFWU — Return From Window Underflow handler.
            //
            // ISA RM §4.4.5 / §8 RFWU (canonical hardware semantics):
            //   N = (PS.OWB XOR WindowBase) is the per-frame rotation distance:
            //     UF4  → N = 1
            //     UF8  → N = 2
            //     UF12 → N = 3
            //   WindowBase ← (WindowBase - N) mod NWINDOWS
            //   s = WindowBase  (new WB)
            //   for i in 0..N: WindowStart[s + i] ← 1
            //   PS.EXCM ← 0
            //   PC ← EPC1
            //
            // Plan 1.5: generalised from UF4-only to handle UF8/12 by computing
            // N from PS.OWB. The OWB is set on underflow entry to the original
            // WindowBase before rotation; the difference modulo 16 gives N.
            Rfwu => {
                let wb_cur = self.regs.windowbase();
                let owb = self.ps.owb();
                let n = ((owb as i32 - wb_cur as i32).rem_euclid(16) as u8).max(1);
                let wb_new = (wb_cur as i32 - n as i32).rem_euclid(16) as u8;
                self.regs.set_windowbase(wb_new);
                for i in 0..n {
                    let bit = (wb_new + i) & 0x0F;
                    self.regs.set_windowstart_bit(bit, true);
                }
                self.ps.set_excm(false);
                self.pc = self.sr.read(EPC1);
            }
  • [ ] Step 3 (Branch B — Rfwu is Rfwu { level } or similar):

If the decoder emits a level field, use it directly. Replace the arm with:

            Rfwu { level } => {
                let n = level / 4;  // level encoded as 4/8/12 → N=1/2/3
                let wb_cur = self.regs.windowbase();
                let wb_new = (wb_cur as i32 - n as i32).rem_euclid(16) as u8;
                self.regs.set_windowbase(wb_new);
                for i in 0..n {
                    let bit = (wb_new + i) & 0x0F;
                    self.regs.set_windowstart_bit(bit, true);
                }
                self.ps.set_excm(false);
                self.pc = self.sr.read(EPC1);
            }

The implementer must inspect the decoder in Step 1 to determine which branch (A or B) applies, then implement only that branch.

  • [ ] Step 4: Write a UF8 oracle test

Add to crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs after retw_window_underflow_uf4 (around line 1067):

// ── H7.12: RETW window underflow UF8 (CALL8 + RETW chain) (Plan 1.5) ─────────

/// CALL8 + ENTRY + RETW where the RETW underflows to a non-live frame at
/// distance 8 from current WB.  Verifies UF8 vector handler entry and RFWU
/// restoration of WB by N=2 (rather than the UF4-only N=1 of Plan 1).
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn retw_window_underflow_uf8() -> OracleCase {
    // Test program: similar to retw_window_underflow_uf4 but using CALL8/ENTRY
    // with a 32-byte frame.  The implementer must hand-encode CALL8/ENTRY/RETW
    // bytes — refer to xtensa_exec.rs for the encoding.
    //
    // Expected end state after RFWU exits the UF8 handler:
    //   - WindowBase rotated back by 2 (N=2 for UF8).
    //   - WindowStart bits at WB and WB+1 are set (the reloaded frames).
    //   - PS.EXCM == 0.
    //
    // Implementation note: the canonical structure follows the UF4 test pattern
    // line-by-line; substitute CALL8 (op2=5) for CALL4 (op2=4) and adjust the
    // ENTRY frame size from 16 to 32 bytes.
    todo!("hand-encode CALL8 + ENTRY 32 + RETW; pattern matches retw_window_underflow_uf4");
}

The implementer fills in the actual encoding by mirroring retw_window_underflow_uf4 (line 1017) with CALL8 instead of CALL4. The structure is identical; only the call instruction encoding and frame size differ. Cross-reference crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs for CALL8 encoding examples.

  • [ ] Step 5: Implement Step 4's test fully (no todo!)

Look at retw_window_underflow_uf4 at line 1017 for the structure. CALL8 encoding: op2=5 (CALL8) instead of op2=4 (CALL4); offset and target same shape. ENTRY frame size: bits 22:0 of ENTRY hold the frame size; for 32 bytes the value is 32 (not the bit-shifted form for ENTRY's encoding — verify via assembler: entry a1, 32).

Use the assembler to generate canonical bytes:

mkdir -p /tmp/uf8_probe && cd /tmp/uf8_probe
cat > prog.s <<'EOF'
.text
_start:
    call8 callee
    break 1, 15
callee:
    entry a1, 32
    retw
EOF
xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as -o prog.o prog.s
xtensa-esp32s3-elf-objdump -d prog.o

Use the bytes from objdump in the test.

  • [ ] Step 6: Run on sim; expect either FAIL (if RFWU was UF4-only) or PASS (if generalised correctly)
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle retw_window_underflow_uf8_sim

Expected: PASS after the RFWU generalisation.

  • [ ] Step 7: Run on HW
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  retw_window_underflow_uf8 -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: all variants PASS.

  • [ ] Step 8: Add a UF12 oracle test using the same pattern
