docs.title: Task 9: MOVSP register spill
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs:1038-1053 (Movsp { at, as_ } arm) - Test: crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs (new oracle test)

Background: When MOVSP detects a live adjacent frame (WindowStart[(WB+1) & 0xF] == 1), it must spill the next frame's registers to memory at [a[at] - 16, a[at]) per the windowed ABI before performing the move. Plan 1 raised AllocaCause instead. The fix implements the full spill, then completes the move.

Reference: ESP-IDF xtensa-rt's _WindowSpill macro (MIT-licensed, design doc §7.6 reference rule allows reading).

  • [ ] Step 1: Read the reference spill semantics

The canonical spill sequence per Xtensa ABI: 1. The "next" frame (WB+1) holds 4 logical AR slots (a0..a3 of that frame, which are physical AR (WB+1)4 .. (WB+1)4+3). 2. The new SP (in a[at]) is the destination's stack pointer; the old SP is in a[as_]. 3. The spill writes physical AR (WB+1)4 .. (WB+1)4+3 to memory at [a[at] - 16, a[at] - 12, a[at] - 8, a[at] - 4] respectively. This is per-Xtensa convention: the next frame's a0..a3 are saved into the destination stack frame's "spill slots" at negative offsets from the new SP. 4. After spill, the simple register move proceeds: a[at] = a[as_] (in current WB). 5. The WindowStart[(WB+1) & 0xF] bit is cleared (the frame is now spilled to memory and no longer live in the register file).

The plan implements exactly this.

  • [ ] Step 2: Write the failing test (MOVSP with live adjacent frame, requiring spill)

Append to crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs after movsp_safe_path (line 1147):

// ── H7.13: MOVSP spill path (adjacent frame IS live) (Plan 1.5) ─────────────

/// MOVSP at, as_: spills the (WB+1) physical AR frame to memory before the
/// move when WS[(WB+1)%16] == 1 (spill path).
///
/// Setup:
///   WB=0, WS=0x0003 (bits 0 and 1 set: frame at WB=0 AND frame at WB+1=1 are live).
///   a4 (current SP, will be moved into a3 = new SP) = SP_NEW = some safe scratch addr.
///   Pre-load physical AR4..AR7 (the WB+1 frame's a0..a3) with sentinels:
///     ar4 = 0xAAAAAAAA, ar5 = 0xBBBBBBBB, ar6 = 0xCCCCCCCC, ar7 = 0xDDDDDDDD.
///
/// Expected post-execution:
///   - a3 (logical, current WB=0) = SP_NEW.
///   - WS bit 1 cleared.
///   - Memory at [SP_NEW - 16 .. SP_NEW - 4] = the four sentinels.
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn movsp_spill_path() -> OracleCase {
    // MOVSP a3, a4: w=0x001430.
    // SP_NEW = IRAM_BASE + 0x1100 (DRAM scratch with at least 16 bytes preceding).
    const SP_NEW: u32 = IRAM_BASE + 0x1100;

    OracleCase::asm(".word 0x001430")
        .setup(|st| {
            st.write_windowstart(0x0003); // bits 0 and 1 set
            st.write_reg("a4", SP_NEW);
            // Set up the spill source frame (physical AR4..AR7) via direct AR
            // writes — these are NOT logical aN at the current WB.
            // The runner exposes physical access via write_register("ar4", ...)
            // (Plan 1.5 fix #2 added this through write_logical_ar; for direct
            // physical writes use write_register with "arN" name).
            //
            // (NOTE: this requires that write_register("ar4", ...) works on
            // both sim and HW. Sim: cpu.regs.set_physical(4, val). HW: OpenOCD
            // may expose ar4..ar63 directly.)
            //
            // For the sim path, OracleState doesn't currently expose physical
            // AR writes — we add a write_physical_ar helper alongside
            // write_reg to make this test runnable.
        })
        .capture_mem(&[SP_NEW - 16, SP_NEW - 12, SP_NEW - 8, SP_NEW - 4])
        .expect(|st| {
            st.assert_reg("a3", SP_NEW);
            // WS bit 1 cleared by spill.
            st.assert_windowstart(0x0001);
            st.assert_mem(SP_NEW - 16, 0xAAAAAAAA);
            st.assert_mem(SP_NEW - 12, 0xBBBBBBBB);
            st.assert_mem(SP_NEW - 8, 0xCCCCCCCC);
            st.assert_mem(SP_NEW - 4, 0xDDDDDDDD);
        })
}

This test references physical AR write — see Step 3 for the helper.

  • [ ] Step 3: Add write_physical_ar helper to OracleState

In crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs, add to OracleState:

    /// Pre-execution physical AR writes, keyed by physical index 0..=63.
    pub physical_ar: HashMap<u8, u32>,

Add the helper:

    /// Write physical AR `phys_idx` (0..=63) before execution.
    pub fn write_physical_ar(&mut self, phys_idx: u8, val: u32) {
        assert!(phys_idx < 64, "write_physical_ar: phys_idx {phys_idx} out of 0..=63");
        self.physical_ar.insert(phys_idx, val);
    }

In capture_sim_state, apply physical AR writes before the logical AR loop:

    for (&phys_idx, &val) in &init_state.physical_ar {
        cpu.regs.set_physical(phys_idx as usize, val);
    }

In capture_hw_state, apply physical AR writes via OpenOCD's arN register names. Add this loop after the WB/WS apply block (after line ~982):

    for (&phys_idx, &val) in &init_state.physical_ar {
        let name = format!("ar{phys_idx}");
        oc.write_register(&name, val)
            .unwrap_or_else(|e| panic!("run_hw: write_register({name}) failed: {e}"));
    }

If OpenOCD doesn't expose arN directly (probed in Task 2 Step 3), use the same WB-rotation pattern as write_logical_ar.

  • [ ] Step 4: Update the test to use write_physical_ar

Modify the test setup in Step 2 to use the new helper:

        .setup(|st| {
            st.write_windowstart(0x0003);
            st.write_reg("a4", SP_NEW);
            st.write_physical_ar(4, 0xAAAAAAAA);
            st.write_physical_ar(5, 0xBBBBBBBB);
            st.write_physical_ar(6, 0xCCCCCCCC);
            st.write_physical_ar(7, 0xDDDDDDDD);
        })
  • [ ] Step 5: Run on sim; expect FAIL
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle movsp_spill_path_sim

Expected: FAIL — the current MOVSP implementation raises AllocaCause instead of spilling.

  • [ ] Step 6: Implement the spill in MOVSP

In crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs:1038-1053, replace:

            Movsp { at, as_ } => {
                let wb = self.regs.windowbase();
                let next_idx = wb.wrapping_add(1) & 0x0F;

                if self.regs.windowstart_bit(next_idx) {
                    // Adjacent frame is live — spill path required.
                    // Plan 1: raise AllocaCause (EXCCAUSE=5) via the general exception path.
                    // TODO(plan2): implement register spill instead.
                    return self.raise_general_exception(5);
                }

                // Safe path: adjacent frame is free, simple register move.
                let v = self.regs.read_logical(as_);
                self.regs.write_logical(at, v);
                self.pc = self.pc.wrapping_add(len);
            }

with:

            // MOVSP at, as_ — Plan 1.5 full spill implementation.
            //
            // ISA RM §8 MOVSP semantics:
            //   if WindowStart[(WB+1) & 0xF] == 1 (adjacent frame live):
            //     spill physical AR ((WB+1)*4 .. (WB+1)*4+3) to memory at
            //     [a[at] - 16, a[at] - 4] respectively (Xtensa windowed ABI).
            //     Clear WindowStart[(WB+1) & 0xF].
            //   Then unconditionally: a[at] = a[as_]; PC += len.
            //
            // Reference: ESP-IDF xtensa-rt _WindowSpill macro (MIT-licensed,
            // design doc §7.6 reference rule).
            Movsp { at, as_ } => {
                let wb = self.regs.windowbase();
                let next_idx = wb.wrapping_add(1) & 0x0F;
                let new_sp = self.regs.read_logical(as_);

                if self.regs.windowstart_bit(next_idx) {
                    // Spill the next frame's physical AR slots to memory
                    // at [new_sp - 16, new_sp - 12, new_sp - 8, new_sp - 4].
                    let next_phys_base = (next_idx as usize) * 4;
                    for i in 0..4u32 {
                        let phys = (next_phys_base + i as usize) & 0x3F;
                        let val = self.regs.physical(phys);
                        let addr = new_sp.wrapping_sub(16).wrapping_add(i * 4) as u64;
                        bus.write_u32(addr, val)?;
                    }
                    // Clear the live bit for the now-spilled frame.
                    self.regs.set_windowstart_bit(next_idx, false);
                }

                self.regs.write_logical(at, new_sp);
                self.pc = self.pc.wrapping_add(len);
            }

Also remove the TODO(plan2): comments at lines 1034 and 1045.

  • [ ] Step 7: Run on sim; expect PASS
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle movsp_spill_path_sim

Expected: PASS.

  • [ ] Step 8: Run the existing movsp_safe_path test to confirm safe path still works
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle movsp_safe_path_sim
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  movsp_safe_path -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: all variants PASS.

  • [ ] Step 9: Run the new test on HW
cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  movsp_spill_path -- --ignored --test-threads=1

Expected: _hw and _diff PASS. If _hw diverges, cross-check the spill ABI against ESP-IDF xtensa-rt _WindowSpill to confirm the offset convention (could be [new_sp..new_sp+12] instead of [new_sp-16..new_sp-4] depending on which direction the stack grows for the spill area).

  • [ ] Step 10: Confirm TODO(plan2) count is now 0
grep -c "TODO(plan2)" crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs

Expected: 0. If non-zero, find and remove the remaining markers.

  • [ ] Step 11: Update Known Gaps

Edit docs/case_study_esp32s3_plan1.md and remove:

**MOVSP exception path.** The instruction is implemented and raises `AllocaCause` (EXCCAUSE=5), but the full register-spill behavior (spilling the window to a stack frame before the exception returns) is deferred to Plan 2, which will add proper stack modeling.
  • [ ] Step 12: Run sim + HW bank
cargo test --workspace \
  --exclude firmware --exclude firmware-ci-fixture --exclude riscv-ci-fixture \
  2>&1 | tail -5

cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --features hw-oracle \
  -- --ignored --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: sim count + 1; HW count + 2.

  • [ ] Step 13: Commit
git add crates/core/src/cpu/xtensa_lx7.rs crates/hw-oracle/src/lib.rs crates/hw-oracle/tests/oracles.rs docs/case_study_esp32s3_plan1.md
git commit -m "fix(xtensa): MOVSP full spill path

Plan 1.5: MOVSP now spills the (WB+1) physical AR frame to memory at
[new_sp-16..new_sp-4] when WS[(WB+1)&0xF] == 1, then clears the WS bit
and completes the move. Closes the AllocaCause workaround from Plan 1.
Reference: ESP-IDF xtensa-rt _WindowSpill macro. Adds
movsp_spill_path oracle test and OracleState::write_physical_ar helper.
Removes both TODO(plan2) markers from xtensa_lx7.rs."

### What changed

  • [ ] Step 4: Add a Plan 1.5 section to docs/hw-oracle-runner-setup.md

Append to docs/hw-oracle-runner-setup.md:

## Plan 1.5 runner additions

The HW runner gained three named capabilities in Plan 1.5:

1. **Per-SR access-form dispatch.** Some Xtensa SRs require `xtensa rsr <name>` /
   `xtensa wsr <name>` rather than `reg <name>`. `OpenOcd::read_sr` / `write_sr`
   probe both forms once per SR per session and cache the working one. Setup
   closures' SR writes (via `OracleState::write_sr` and helpers like
   `write_epc1`, `write_intenable`, etc.) all route through the dispatch.
2. **Windowed-register translation.** `OpenOcd::read_logical_ar(idx)` and
   `write_logical_ar(idx, val)` compute `phys = ((wb << 2) + idx) & 0x3F`
   from the current `WindowBase` and route to the corresponding physical AR.
   `capture_hw_state` calls these for end-state register capture.
3. **VECBASE write sequencer.** `OpenOcd::set_vecbase(addr)` wraps a halt →
   write → halt sequence so the next exception dispatch picks up the new
   value. `capture_hw_state`'s SR setup loop special-cases VECBASE through
   this method.

The runner also forces `WindowBase` / `WindowStart` BEFORE applying the
test's logical-AR setup writes (Plan 1.5 fix #2), so tests that pre-load
`WindowBase` via `OracleState::write_windowbase` see their setup registers
land in the correct physical slot.

docs.title: Plan 4 — ESP32-S3 I²C + TMP102 sensor demo Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For agentic workers: REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (- [ ]) syntax for tracking.

Goal: End-to-end demo where an unmodified esp-hal firmware reads a simulated TMP102 over I²C, prints temperature over USB-Serial-JTAG, and toggles GPIO2 above 30 °C — running identically in the LabWired simulator and on a real ESP32-S3-Zero (with HW-oracle parity validation at the I²C controller register level).

Architecture: Add a new ESP32-S3 I²C0 controller Peripheral modeling the command-list engine; add a TMP102 I2cDevice with drifting temperature; wire them together in configure_xtensa_esp32s3. The firmware uses esp-hal::i2c::master::I2c::write_read exactly as on real silicon. Tests at four layers: per-module unit tests, sim end-to-end test mirroring e2e_blinky.rs, hw-oracle controller-register-level tests, and a manual runbook for L4 with a physical sensor.

Tech Stack: Rust (workspace labwired-core), esp-hal 1.1.x, esp-println JTAG output, critical-section, hw-oracle harness from PR #82, xtensa-esp-elf toolchain (+esp rustup channel) for firmware builds.

Spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-30-plan-4-i2c-tmp102-design.md

docs.title: Task 1: TMP102 device — pointer write + temperature read (no drift)
docs.text:
Files: - Create: crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/tmp102.rs - Modify: crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/mod.rs

  • [ ] Step 1.1: Write the failing test

Create crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/tmp102.rs:

// LabWired - Firmware Simulation Platform
// Copyright (C) 2026 Andrii Shylenko
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT

//! TMP102 I²C temperature sensor as an `I2cDevice`.
//!
//! Per Texas Instruments TMP102 datasheet:
//! - 7-bit address 0x48 (ADD0 = GND).
//! - Pointer register selects which 16-bit data register subsequent reads/writes target.
//! - Temperature register is 12-bit, left-justified into a 16-bit big-endian value
//!   (MSB returned first), with 1 LSB = 0.0625 °C.
//!
//! For the simulator we only model the temperature register's read path plus
//! pointer tracking. Drift behavior is added in Task 2.

use crate::peripherals::i2c::I2cDevice;

const TMP102_ADDR: u8 = 0x48;
const TMP_INITIAL: i16 = 0x1900; // 25.0 °C left-justified in 12-bit/16-bit

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Tmp102 {
    pointer: u8,
    temp_raw: i16,
    /// Phase tracker: 0 = next read returns MSB; 1 = next read returns LSB.
    /// Reset to 0 on `start()`.
    read_phase: u8,
    /// Tracks how many writes have occurred since `start()` so the first
    /// post-start write sets the pointer and subsequent writes are absorbed
    /// into config/T_LOW/T_HIGH (ignored for the demo).
    writes_since_start: u32,
}

impl Tmp102 {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        Self {
            pointer: 0,
            temp_raw: TMP_INITIAL,
            read_phase: 0,
            writes_since_start: 0,
        }
    }
}

impl Default for Tmp102 {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self::new()
    }
}

impl I2cDevice for Tmp102 {
    fn address(&self) -> u8 {
        TMP102_ADDR
    }

    fn write(&mut self, data: u8) {
        if self.writes_since_start == 0 {
            // First write after start sets the pointer register.
            self.pointer = data & 0x03;
        }
        // Subsequent writes (e.g. config bytes) are accepted and ignored —
        // the demo never writes those registers.
        self.writes_since_start = self.writes_since_start.saturating_add(1);
    }

    fn read(&mut self) -> u8 {
        // For now, only the temperature register (pointer 0) is exercised.
        let value: u16 = match self.pointer {
            0 => self.temp_raw as u16,
            1 => 0x60A0,                 // CONFIG canned value
            2 => 0x4B00,                 // T_LOW = 75 °C
            3 => 0x5000,                 // T_HIGH = 80 °C
            _ => 0,                      // unreachable due to mask in write()
        };
        let byte = if self.read_phase == 0 {
            (value >> 8) as u8
        } else {
            (value & 0xFF) as u8
        };
        self.read_phase ^= 1;
        byte
    }

    fn start(&mut self) {
        self.read_phase = 0;
        self.writes_since_start = 0;
    }
}

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn address_is_0x48() {
        let dev = Tmp102::new();
        assert_eq!(dev.address(), 0x48);
    }

    #[test]
    fn pointer_set_by_first_write_after_start() {
        let mut dev = Tmp102::new();
        dev.start();
        dev.write(0x01); // pointer ← 0x01 (CONFIG)
        assert_eq!(dev.pointer, 0x01);
    }

    #[test]
    fn temperature_read_returns_msb_then_lsb() {
        let mut dev = Tmp102::new();
        dev.start();
        let msb = dev.read();
        let lsb = dev.read();
        assert_eq!(msb, 0x19);
        assert_eq!(lsb, 0x00);
    }

    #[test]
    fn read_phase_resets_on_start() {
        let mut dev = Tmp102::new();
        dev.start();
        let _ = dev.read(); // advance phase to 1
        dev.start();        // reset phase
        let msb = dev.read();
        assert_eq!(msb, 0x19);
    }
}

docs.title: Task 2: TMP102 drift behavior
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/tmp102.rs

  • [ ] Step 2.1: Write the failing tests

Append to the mod tests block in crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/tmp102.rs:

    #[test]
    fn drift_increments_after_full_read() {
        let mut dev = Tmp102::new();
        dev.start();
        let _msb = dev.read();
        let _lsb = dev.read(); // full read pair → tick
        // Internal raw should have advanced by 0x80 (0.5 °C).
        assert_eq!(dev.temp_raw, 0x1980);
    }

    #[test]
    fn drift_wraps_at_35c_back_to_20c() {
        let mut dev = Tmp102::new();
        dev.temp_raw = 0x2300; // 35.0 °C
        dev.start();
        let _ = dev.read();
        let _ = dev.read(); // tick → 35.5 °C → wraps to 20.0 °C
        assert_eq!(dev.temp_raw, 0x1400);
    }

    #[test]
    fn partial_read_does_not_increment() {
        let mut dev = Tmp102::new();
        dev.start();
        let _ = dev.read(); // only MSB; phase=1
        // No tick yet — temp_raw must be unchanged.
        assert_eq!(dev.temp_raw, 0x1900);
    }
  • [ ] Step 2.2: Run tests — they should fail

Run: cargo test -p labwired-core --lib peripherals::esp32s3::tmp102::tests::drift 2>&1 | tail -10

Expected: drift_increments_after_full_read and drift_wraps_at_35c_back_to_20c FAIL with assertion left == right errors (drift not implemented yet).

  • [ ] Step 2.3: Implement drift in read()

Replace the read() body in crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/tmp102.rs:

    fn read(&mut self) -> u8 {
        let value: u16 = match self.pointer {
            0 => self.temp_raw as u16,
            1 => 0x60A0,
            2 => 0x4B00,
            3 => 0x5000,
            _ => 0,
        };
        let byte = if self.read_phase == 0 {
            (value >> 8) as u8
        } else {
            (value & 0xFF) as u8
        };
        self.read_phase ^= 1;
        // Tick drift only at the end of a full MSB+LSB pair, and only when
        // reading the temperature register.
        if self.read_phase == 0 && self.pointer == 0 {
            self.temp_raw = self.temp_raw.wrapping_add(0x80); // +0.5 °C
            if self.temp_raw > 0x2300 {
                self.temp_raw = 0x1400; // wrap to 20 °C
            }
        }
        byte
    }
  • [ ] Step 2.4: Run tests — they should pass

Run: cargo test -p labwired-core --lib peripherals::esp32s3::tmp102 2>&1 | tail -10

Expected: all tmp102 tests PASS.

git commit -m "feat(esp32s3): TMP102 drift +0.5C/read with 35->20 wrap (Plan 4 task 2)

Temperature drifts by 0x80 (0.5 °C) after each full MSB+LSB read pair
and wraps from 35 °C back to 20 °C. Partial reads (MSB only) do not
advance the counter."

docs.title: Task 3: I²C controller — register file
docs.text:
Files: - Create: crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs - Modify: crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/mod.rs

  • [ ] Step 3.1: Write the register file + read/write tests

Create crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs:

// LabWired - Firmware Simulation Platform
// Copyright (C) 2026 Andrii Shylenko
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT

//! ESP32-S3 I²C0 controller — command-list engine.
//!
//! Mapped at base 0x6001_3000 with size 4 KiB. See ESP32-S3 TRM §29.
//!
//! ## Register subset modeled
//!
//! | Offset | Name        | Notes                                          |
//! |--------|-------------|------------------------------------------------|
//! | 0x04   | CTR         | TRANS_START at bit 5                           |
//! | 0x10   | SLAVE_ADDR  | 7-bit address in [6:0]                         |
//! | 0x18   | FIFO_DATA   | Write→TX FIFO, read→pop RX FIFO                |
//! | 0x1C   | FIFO_CONF   | Reset bits accept and clear (no behavior)      |
//! | 0x20   | INT_RAW     | Bit 7 = TRANS_COMPLETE; bit 11 = NACK          |
//! | 0x24   | INT_CLR     | Write 1 to clear matching INT_RAW bits         |
//! | 0x28   | INT_ENA     | Enable mask                                    |
//! | 0x2C   | INT_ST      | INT_RAW & INT_ENA                              |
//! | 0x44   | FIFO_ST     | TX/RX FIFO levels                              |
//! | 0x58.. | CMD0..CMD15 | 14-bit command words (16 entries × 4 B)        |
//!
//! All other offsets accept writes silently and read 0.

use crate::peripherals::i2c::I2cDevice;
use crate::{Peripheral, PeripheralTickResult, SimResult, SimulationError};

pub const I2C0_BASE: u32 = 0x6001_3000;
pub const I2C0_SIZE: u64 = 0x1000;

/// ESP32-S3 I_I2C_EXT0_INT_SOURCE per TRM §9.4 table 9-1.
pub const I2C0_INTR_SOURCE_ID: u32 = 49;

const REG_CTR: u64 = 0x04;
const REG_SLAVE_ADDR: u64 = 0x10;
const REG_FIFO_DATA: u64 = 0x18;
const REG_FIFO_CONF: u64 = 0x1C;
const REG_INT_RAW: u64 = 0x20;
const REG_INT_CLR: u64 = 0x24;
const REG_INT_ENA: u64 = 0x28;
const REG_INT_ST: u64 = 0x2C;
const REG_FIFO_ST: u64 = 0x44;
const REG_CMD0: u64 = 0x58;
const REG_CMD15: u64 = 0x94;

const CTR_TRANS_START_BIT: u32 = 1 << 5;

pub const INT_TRANS_COMPLETE: u32 = 1 << 7;
pub const INT_NACK: u32 = 1 << 11;

const NUM_CMDS: usize = 16;
const FIFO_CAPACITY: usize = 32;

// NOTE: do NOT use `#[derive(Debug)]` — `Box<dyn I2cDevice>` is not Debug.
// We provide a manual impl below that reports field summaries.
pub struct Esp32s3I2c {
    ctr: u32,
    slave_addr: u32,
    int_raw: u32,
    int_ena: u32,
    fifo_conf: u32,
    cmds: [u32; NUM_CMDS],
    tx_fifo: std::collections::VecDeque<u8>,
    rx_fifo: std::collections::VecDeque<u8>,
    slaves: Vec<Box<dyn I2cDevice>>,
    /// Set when a command-list run sets TRANS_COMPLETE & INT_ENA has it.
    /// Drained by `tick()` into the interrupt-matrix source aggregation.
    irq_pending: bool,
}

impl std::fmt::Debug for Esp32s3I2c {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
        f.debug_struct("Esp32s3I2c")
            .field("ctr", &format_args!("0x{:08x}", self.ctr))
            .field("slave_addr", &format_args!("0x{:08x}", self.slave_addr))
            .field("int_raw", &format_args!("0x{:08x}", self.int_raw))
            .field("int_ena", &format_args!("0x{:08x}", self.int_ena))
            .field("tx_fifo_len", &self.tx_fifo.len())
            .field("rx_fifo_len", &self.rx_fifo.len())
            .field("slaves_count", &self.slaves.len())
            .field("irq_pending", &self.irq_pending)
            .finish()
    }
}

impl Esp32s3I2c {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        Self {
            ctr: 0,
            slave_addr: 0,
            int_raw: 0,
            int_ena: 0,
            fifo_conf: 0,
            cmds: [0; NUM_CMDS],
            tx_fifo: std::collections::VecDeque::with_capacity(FIFO_CAPACITY),
            rx_fifo: std::collections::VecDeque::with_capacity(FIFO_CAPACITY),
            slaves: Vec::new(),
            irq_pending: false,
        }
    }

    /// Attach an `I2cDevice` slave. Slaves are matched by address bits at
    /// transaction time; later additions take precedence on duplicate addresses.
    pub fn attach_slave(&mut self, slave: Box<dyn I2cDevice>) {
        self.slaves.push(slave);
    }

    fn fifo_status(&self) -> u32 {
        // Bits [4:0] = TX FIFO level, bits [12:8] = RX FIFO level.
        ((self.tx_fifo.len() as u32) & 0x1F) | (((self.rx_fifo.len() as u32) & 0x1F) << 8)
    }
}

impl Default for Esp32s3I2c {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self::new()
    }
}

impl Peripheral for Esp32s3I2c {
    fn read(&self, _offset: u64) -> SimResult<u8> {
        // Byte reads aren't used by esp-hal's I2C driver; route everything
        // through read_u32. Returning 0 for stray byte reads is harmless.
        Ok(0)
    }

    fn read_u32(&self, offset: u64) -> SimResult<u32> {
        let v = match offset {
            REG_CTR => self.ctr,
            REG_SLAVE_ADDR => self.slave_addr,
            REG_FIFO_DATA => 0, // see below — RX pop is handled in write_u32 path
            REG_FIFO_CONF => self.fifo_conf,
            REG_INT_RAW => self.int_raw,
            REG_INT_CLR => 0,
            REG_INT_ENA => self.int_ena,
            REG_INT_ST => self.int_raw & self.int_ena,
            REG_FIFO_ST => self.fifo_status(),
            REG_CMD0..=REG_CMD15 => {
                let idx = ((offset - REG_CMD0) / 4) as usize;
                self.cmds.get(idx).copied().unwrap_or(0)
            }
            _ => 0,
        };
        Ok(v)
    }

    fn write(&mut self, _offset: u64, _value: u8) -> SimResult<()> {
        // Byte writes ignored — the esp-hal driver writes whole words.
        Ok(())
    }

    fn write_u32(&mut self, offset: u64, value: u32) -> SimResult<()> {
        match offset {
            REG_CTR => {
                self.ctr = value;
                if value & CTR_TRANS_START_BIT != 0 {
                    self.run_command_list();
                    // Auto-clear TRANS_START like real silicon.
                    self.ctr &= !CTR_TRANS_START_BIT;
                }
            }
            REG_SLAVE_ADDR => self.slave_addr = value,
            REG_FIFO_DATA => {
                // Byte 0 of value goes into TX FIFO (per esp-hal usage).
                if self.tx_fifo.len() < FIFO_CAPACITY {
                    self.tx_fifo.push_back((value & 0xFF) as u8);
                }
            }
            REG_FIFO_CONF => {
                self.fifo_conf = value;
                // Bit 12 = RX_FIFO_RST; bit 13 = TX_FIFO_RST. Self-clearing.
                if value & (1 << 12) != 0 {
                    self.rx_fifo.clear();
                }
                if value & (1 << 13) != 0 {
                    self.tx_fifo.clear();
                }
                self.fifo_conf &= !((1 << 12) | (1 << 13));
            }
            REG_INT_CLR => self.int_raw &= !value,
            REG_INT_ENA => self.int_ena = value,
            REG_CMD0..=REG_CMD15 => {
                let idx = ((offset - REG_CMD0) / 4) as usize;
                if let Some(slot) = self.cmds.get_mut(idx) {
                    *slot = value;
                }
            }
            _ => {} // Accept-and-ignore (timing regs, etc.)
        }
        Ok(())
    }

    fn tick(&mut self) -> PeripheralTickResult {
        let mut explicit = Vec::new();
        if self.irq_pending {
            explicit.push(I2C0_INTR_SOURCE_ID);
            self.irq_pending = false;
        }
        PeripheralTickResult {
            explicit_irqs: if explicit.is_empty() { None } else { Some(explicit) },
            ..Default::default()
        }
    }

    fn as_any(&self) -> Option<&dyn std::any::Any> {
        Some(self)
    }

    fn as_any_mut(&mut self) -> Option<&mut dyn std::any::Any> {
        Some(self)
    }
}

impl Esp32s3I2c {
    /// Walk CMD0..CMD15 from the start, executing each command. Stub for
    /// task 3 — writes set TRANS_COMPLETE only, no slave interaction yet.
    fn run_command_list(&mut self) {
        // Filled in by Tasks 5 and 6.
        self.int_raw |= INT_TRANS_COMPLETE;
        if self.int_ena & INT_TRANS_COMPLETE != 0 {
            self.irq_pending = true;
        }
        // Suppress unused-variable warnings until task 5 fills in the body.
        let _ = SimulationError::NotImplemented(String::new()); // satisfies import use
    }
}

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn ctr_round_trip() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(REG_CTR, 0x0000_0010).unwrap(); // arbitrary, no TRANS_START
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_CTR).unwrap(), 0x0000_0010);
    }

    #[test]
    fn slave_addr_round_trip() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(REG_SLAVE_ADDR, 0x48).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_SLAVE_ADDR).unwrap(), 0x48);
    }

    #[test]
    fn cmd_registers_round_trip() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0, 0x0000_0800).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD15, 0x0000_2000).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_CMD0).unwrap(), 0x0000_0800);
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_CMD15).unwrap(), 0x0000_2000);
    }

    #[test]
    fn unmapped_offsets_read_zero_and_accept_writes() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(0xFFC, 0xDEAD_BEEF).unwrap(); // last 4-byte slot in 4 KiB
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(0xFFC).unwrap(), 0);
    }

    #[test]
    fn fifo_status_reflects_pushes() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0xAA).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0xBB).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0xCC).unwrap();
        let st = p.read_u32(REG_FIFO_ST).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(st & 0x1F, 3, "TX level should reflect 3 pushes");
    }

    #[test]
    fn fifo_reset_bits_clear_fifos() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0x11).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0x22).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_CONF, 1 << 13).unwrap(); // TX_FIFO_RST
        let st = p.read_u32(REG_FIFO_ST).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(st & 0x1F, 0);
    }

    #[test]
    fn int_clr_clears_specified_bits() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.int_raw = INT_TRANS_COMPLETE | INT_NACK;
        p.write_u32(REG_INT_CLR, INT_NACK).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_INT_RAW).unwrap(), INT_TRANS_COMPLETE);
    }

    #[test]
    fn int_st_masks_with_int_ena() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.int_raw = INT_TRANS_COMPLETE | INT_NACK;
        p.write_u32(REG_INT_ENA, INT_TRANS_COMPLETE).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_INT_ST).unwrap(), INT_TRANS_COMPLETE);
    }
}

Adds Esp32s3I2c Peripheral covering CTR / SLAVE_ADDR / FIFO_DATA /

docs.title: Task 4: Command-list executor — RSTART, STOP, END terminators (no slaves yet)
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs

  • [ ] Step 4.1: Write the failing tests

Append to the mod tests block in crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs:

    /// Encode a 14-bit command word: opcode | byte_num.
    fn cmd(opcode: u8, byte_num: u8) -> u32 {
        ((opcode as u32 & 0x7) << 11) | (byte_num as u32)
    }

    const CMD_RSTART: u8 = 0;
    const CMD_WRITE: u8 = 1;
    const CMD_READ: u8 = 2;
    const CMD_STOP: u8 = 3;
    const CMD_END: u8 = 4;

    #[test]
    fn empty_command_list_with_end_completes() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0, cmd(CMD_END, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CTR, CTR_TRANS_START_BIT).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(
            p.read_u32(REG_INT_RAW).unwrap() & INT_TRANS_COMPLETE,
            INT_TRANS_COMPLETE
        );
    }

    #[test]
    fn rstart_then_stop_completes() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0, cmd(CMD_RSTART, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD1_OFFSET, cmd(CMD_STOP, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CTR, CTR_TRANS_START_BIT).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(
            p.read_u32(REG_INT_RAW).unwrap() & INT_TRANS_COMPLETE,
            INT_TRANS_COMPLETE
        );
    }

    #[test]
    fn trans_start_auto_clears() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0, cmd(CMD_END, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CTR, CTR_TRANS_START_BIT).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_CTR).unwrap() & CTR_TRANS_START_BIT, 0);
    }

Add at top of test module (just after use super::*;):

    const REG_CMD1_OFFSET: u64 = REG_CMD0 + 4;

Run: cargo test -p labwired-core --lib peripherals::esp32s3::i2c::tests 2>&1 | tail -15

Expected: all PASS (the stub still sets TRANS_COMPLETE unconditionally). This confirms the encoding helpers compile. The real distinction comes in tasks 5/6 when WRITE/READ commands need slaves.

  • [ ] Step 4.3: Replace run_command_list with the real executor (terminators only — no WRITE/READ yet)

Replace the run_command_list impl block in crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs:

impl Esp32s3I2c {
    /// Walk CMD0..CMD15 from the start, executing each command in order.
    /// Stops at the first STOP or END terminator. WRITE / READ are
    /// implemented in tasks 5/6.
    fn run_command_list(&mut self) {
        const OP_RSTART: u32 = 0;
        const OP_WRITE: u32 = 1;
        const OP_READ: u32 = 2;
        const OP_STOP: u32 = 3;
        const OP_END: u32 = 4;

        let mut completed = false;
        for &word in &self.cmds {
            let opcode = (word >> 11) & 0x7;
            match opcode {
                OP_RSTART => { /* slave start handled in task 6 */ }
                OP_WRITE => { /* filled in task 6 */ }
                OP_READ => { /* filled in task 6 */ }
                OP_STOP | OP_END => {
                    completed = true;
                    break;
                }
                _ => {
                    // Reserved/unknown opcode — terminate.
                    completed = true;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        let _ = completed; // suppresses unused-variable warning until task 6
        self.int_raw |= INT_TRANS_COMPLETE;
        if self.int_ena & INT_TRANS_COMPLETE != 0 {
            self.irq_pending = true;
        }
    }
}

Remove the let _ = SimulationError::NotImplemented(String::new()); placeholder line.

Also remove the unused SimulationError import — change the top-of-file use block to:

use crate::{Peripheral, PeripheralTickResult, SimResult};

run_command_list now walks CMD0..CMD15 and stops at the first STOP or
END opcode (or unknown opcode, defensively). WRITE/READ slots are
present but no-op until task 6 wires up I2cDevice slaves.

TRANS_START auto-clears after a list run, matching real silicon."

docs.title: Task 5: I²C — TX/RX FIFO READ/WRITE for FIFO_DATA word reads
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs

The read_u32 path currently returns 0 for REG_FIFO_DATA. esp-hal pops bytes by reading FIFO_DATA. Implement RX pop in read_u32. This requires changing the trait method to take &mut self — but Peripheral::read_u32 is &self. Workaround: model RX FIFO as RefCell (or special-case via interior mutability), or change read_u32 to drain the byte by going through as_any_mut. The simpler approach: implement read_u32 for everything else, and have a separate path that the bus invokes for read_u8 of REG_FIFO_DATA that pops the byte.

Looking at how the existing UART models handle this: Uart::read is &self and just returns whatever's at the offset; pop happens because the firmware reads exactly once. Use the same pattern with interior mutability.

  • [ ] Step 5.1: Convert RX FIFO to RefCell<VecDeque<u8>> for read-side pop

Modify the struct and impl:

use std::cell::RefCell;

#[derive(Debug)]
pub struct Esp32s3I2c {
    ctr: u32,
    slave_addr: u32,
    int_raw: u32,
    int_ena: u32,
    fifo_conf: u32,
    cmds: [u32; NUM_CMDS],
    tx_fifo: std::collections::VecDeque<u8>,
    rx_fifo: RefCell<std::collections::VecDeque<u8>>,
    slaves: Vec<Box<dyn I2cDevice>>,
    irq_pending: bool,
}

impl Esp32s3I2c {
    pub fn new() -> Self {
        Self {
            ctr: 0,
            slave_addr: 0,
            int_raw: 0,
            int_ena: 0,
            fifo_conf: 0,
            cmds: [0; NUM_CMDS],
            tx_fifo: std::collections::VecDeque::with_capacity(FIFO_CAPACITY),
            rx_fifo: RefCell::new(std::collections::VecDeque::with_capacity(FIFO_CAPACITY)),
            slaves: Vec::new(),
            irq_pending: false,
        }
    }

    pub fn attach_slave(&mut self, slave: Box<dyn I2cDevice>) {
        self.slaves.push(slave);
    }

    fn fifo_status(&self) -> u32 {
        let rx_len = self.rx_fifo.borrow().len();
        ((self.tx_fifo.len() as u32) & 0x1F) | (((rx_len as u32) & 0x1F) << 8)
    }
}

Update read_u32 to pop from RX FIFO when REG_FIFO_DATA is read:

    fn read_u32(&self, offset: u64) -> SimResult<u32> {
        let v = match offset {
            REG_CTR => self.ctr,
            REG_SLAVE_ADDR => self.slave_addr,
            REG_FIFO_DATA => self.rx_fifo.borrow_mut().pop_front().unwrap_or(0) as u32,
            REG_FIFO_CONF => self.fifo_conf,
            REG_INT_RAW => self.int_raw,
            REG_INT_CLR => 0,
            REG_INT_ENA => self.int_ena,
            REG_INT_ST => self.int_raw & self.int_ena,
            REG_FIFO_ST => self.fifo_status(),
            REG_CMD0..=REG_CMD15 => {
                let idx = ((offset - REG_CMD0) / 4) as usize;
                self.cmds.get(idx).copied().unwrap_or(0)
            }
            _ => 0,
        };
        Ok(v)
    }

In write_u32, replace the existing REG_FIFO_CONF reset path's self.rx_fifo.clear() with self.rx_fifo.borrow_mut().clear().

docs.title: Task 6: I²C — slave attachment + WRITE/READ command execution
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs

  • [ ] Step 6.1: Write the failing tests

Append to the mod tests block:

    use crate::peripherals::esp32s3::tmp102::Tmp102;

    #[test]
    fn write_read_drives_attached_tmp102() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.attach_slave(Box::new(Tmp102::new()));

        // Build the canonical TMP102 read sequence:
        //   RSTART; WRITE 2 (addr+W, pointer=0); RSTART;
        //   WRITE 1 (addr+R); READ 2; STOP.
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0, cmd(CMD_RSTART, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 4, cmd(CMD_WRITE, 2)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 8, cmd(CMD_RSTART, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 12, cmd(CMD_WRITE, 1)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 16, cmd(CMD_READ, 2)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 20, cmd(CMD_STOP, 0)).unwrap();

        // Push TX bytes: addr+W, pointer 0, addr+R.
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0x90).unwrap(); // 0x48 << 1 | W
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0x00).unwrap(); // pointer
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0x91).unwrap(); // 0x48 << 1 | R

        p.write_u32(REG_CTR, CTR_TRANS_START_BIT).unwrap();

        // Two bytes of temperature should now be in RX FIFO: 0x19, 0x00.
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA).unwrap(), 0x19);
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA).unwrap(), 0x00);
        assert_eq!(
            p.read_u32(REG_INT_RAW).unwrap() & INT_TRANS_COMPLETE,
            INT_TRANS_COMPLETE
        );
    }

    #[test]
    fn write_with_unmatched_address_sets_nack_int() {
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        // No slaves attached.
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0, cmd(CMD_RSTART, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 4, cmd(CMD_WRITE, 1)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 8, cmd(CMD_STOP, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0xA0).unwrap(); // some addr+W, no slave
        p.write_u32(REG_CTR, CTR_TRANS_START_BIT).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(
            p.read_u32(REG_INT_RAW).unwrap() & INT_NACK,
            INT_NACK,
            "INT_NACK should fire when no slave matches"
        );
    }

    #[test]
    fn write_then_read_pointer_round_trip() {
        // Set pointer to CONFIG (0x01) via WRITE, then READ should return
        // CONFIG canned value 0x60A0 high byte then low byte.
        let mut p = Esp32s3I2c::new();
        p.attach_slave(Box::new(Tmp102::new()));

        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0, cmd(CMD_RSTART, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 4, cmd(CMD_WRITE, 2)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 8, cmd(CMD_RSTART, 0)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 12, cmd(CMD_WRITE, 1)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 16, cmd(CMD_READ, 2)).unwrap();
        p.write_u32(REG_CMD0 + 20, cmd(CMD_STOP, 0)).unwrap();

        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0x90).unwrap(); // addr+W
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0x01).unwrap(); // pointer = CONFIG
        p.write_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA, 0x91).unwrap(); // addr+R
        p.write_u32(REG_CTR, CTR_TRANS_START_BIT).unwrap();

        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA).unwrap(), 0x60);
        assert_eq!(p.read_u32(REG_FIFO_DATA).unwrap(), 0xA0);
    }

Expected: write_read_drives_attached_tmp102, write_with_unmatched_address_sets_nack_int, and write_then_read_pointer_round_trip FAIL — RX FIFO is empty / NACK not set.

  • [ ] Step 6.3: Implement WRITE / READ in run_command_list

Replace the entire run_command_list body in crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs:

impl Esp32s3I2c {
    /// Walk CMD0..CMD15 from the start, executing each command. A "WRITE"
    /// whose first byte follows an RSTART is interpreted as `(addr<<1)|R/W`
    /// and selects the active slave by address bits [7:1]. Subsequent
    /// WRITE bytes are delivered via `I2cDevice::write`. READ pulls bytes
    /// from the active slave via `I2cDevice::read` and pushes to the RX FIFO.
    fn run_command_list(&mut self) {
        const OP_RSTART: u32 = 0;
        const OP_WRITE: u32 = 1;
        const OP_READ: u32 = 2;
        const OP_STOP: u32 = 3;
        const OP_END: u32 = 4;

        // Index into self.slaves of the currently-selected device, or
        // None if no address has been latched yet (or just after RSTART).
        let mut active: Option<usize> = None;
        let mut expects_addr = true;

        for &word in &self.cmds {
            let opcode = (word >> 11) & 0x7;
            let byte_num = (word & 0xFF) as usize;
            match opcode {
                OP_RSTART => {
                    if let Some(idx) = active {
                        self.slaves[idx].start();
                    }
                    expects_addr = true;
                    active = None;
                }
                OP_WRITE => {
                    for i in 0..byte_num {
                        let b = self.tx_fifo.pop_front().unwrap_or(0);
                        if expects_addr && i == 0 {
                            // First byte of a WRITE following RSTART is addr+R/W.
                            let addr = b >> 1;
                            active = self
                                .slaves
                                .iter()
                                .position(|s| s.address() == addr);
                            if active.is_none() {
                                self.int_raw |= INT_NACK;
                            }
                            expects_addr = false;
                            // Don't deliver the addr byte to the slave's write().
                            continue;
                        }
                        if let Some(idx) = active {
                            self.slaves[idx].write(b);
                        }
                    }
                }
                OP_READ => {
                    for _ in 0..byte_num {
                        let b = if let Some(idx) = active {
                            self.slaves[idx].read()
                        } else {
                            0
                        };
                        let mut rx = self.rx_fifo.borrow_mut();
                        if rx.len() < FIFO_CAPACITY {
                            rx.push_back(b);
                        }
                    }
                }
                OP_STOP => {
                    if let Some(idx) = active {
                        self.slaves[idx].stop();
                    }
                    break;
                }
                OP_END => break,
                _ => break, // reserved opcode — terminate
            }
        }

        self.int_raw |= INT_TRANS_COMPLETE;
        if self.int_ena & (INT_TRANS_COMPLETE | INT_NACK) != 0 {
            self.irq_pending = true;
        }
    }
}
  • [ ] Step 6.4: Run tests — they should pass

Run: cargo test -p labwired-core --lib peripherals::esp32s3::i2c 2>&1 | tail -25

Expected: all 15 tests PASS.

  • [ ] Step 6.5: Commit
git add crates/core/src/peripherals/esp32s3/i2c.rs
git commit -m "feat(esp32s3-i2c): WRITE/READ command execution with slaves (Plan 4 task 6)

run_command_list now interprets WRITE byte_num=N (first byte after
RSTART = addr+R/W → slave selection by address bits, remaining bytes
delivered via I2cDevice::write) and READ byte_num=N (pull from active
slave via I2cDevice::read, push to RX FIFO).

INT_NACK fires when an address has no matching attached slave —
matching real-silicon behavior on a missing peripheral."

    #[test]
    fn configure_registers_i2c0_with_tmp102_attached() {
        let mut bus = SystemBus::new();
        let _wiring =
            configure_xtensa_esp32s3(&mut bus, &Esp32s3Opts::default()).unwrap();

        // I2C0 should be present at 0x6001_3000.
        let names: Vec<_> = bus.peripherals.iter().map(|p| p.id.as_str()).collect();
        assert!(
            names.contains(&"i2c0"),
            "i2c0 missing; have: {names:?}"
        );

        // The attached TMP102 should respond at address 0x48 by setting
        // INT_NACK to 0 after a one-byte write probe.
        let i2c_idx = bus.peripherals.iter().position(|p| p.id == "i2c0").unwrap();
        let i2c_any = bus.peripherals[i2c_idx]
            .dev
            .as_any_mut()
            .expect("i2c0 should expose as_any_mut");
        let i2c = i2c_any
            .downcast_mut::<crate::peripherals::esp32s3::i2c::Esp32s3I2c>()
            .expect("downcast to Esp32s3I2c");

        // Build a probe: RSTART; WRITE 1 (addr+W=0x90); STOP.
        i2c.write_u32(0x58, ((0u32) << 11) | 0).unwrap(); // RSTART
        i2c.write_u32(0x5C, ((1u32) << 11) | 1).unwrap(); // WRITE 1
        i2c.write_u32(0x60, ((3u32) << 11) | 0).unwrap(); // STOP
        i2c.write_u32(0x18, 0x90).unwrap();                // addr+W
        i2c.write_u32(0x04, 1 << 5).unwrap();              // TRANS_START
        let int_raw = i2c.read_u32(0x20).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(
            int_raw & (1 << 11),
            0,
            "TMP102 attached at 0x48 must ACK; got INT_RAW=0x{int_raw:08x}"
        );
    }

  • [ ] Step 8.5: Write the firmware

Create examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/src/main.rs:

//! ESP32-S3 I²C + TMP102 demo for the LabWired simulator (Plan 4).
//!
//! Reads temperature from a TMP102 over I²C0 once per second, prints it
//! over USB-Serial-JTAG, and toggles GPIO2 high when temperature exceeds
//! 30 °C. Runs identically on the simulator (against the simulated TMP102
//! at 0x48) and on real silicon (with a TMP102 wired to GPIO8/GPIO9).

#![no_std]
#![no_main]

use core::cell::RefCell;
use critical_section::Mutex;
use esp_backtrace as _;
use esp_hal::{
    gpio::{Level, Output, OutputConfig},
    handler,
    i2c::master::{Config as I2cConfig, I2c},
    main,
    time::Duration,
    timer::{systimer::SystemTimer, PeriodicTimer},
};
use esp_println::println;

const TMP102_ADDR: u8 = 0x48;
const THRESHOLD_C: f32 = 30.0;

static TICK_FLAG: Mutex<RefCell<bool>> = Mutex::new(RefCell::new(false));
static ALARM: Mutex<RefCell<Option<PeriodicTimer<'static, esp_hal::Blocking>>>> =
    Mutex::new(RefCell::new(None));

#[handler]
fn alarm_isr() {
    critical_section::with(|cs| {
        TICK_FLAG.replace(cs, true);
        if let Some(alarm) = ALARM.borrow_ref_mut(cs).as_mut() {
            alarm.clear_interrupt();
        }
    });
}

#[main]
fn main() -> ! {
    let p = esp_hal::init(esp_hal::Config::default());

    let mut led = Output::new(p.GPIO2, Level::Low, OutputConfig::default());

    let mut i2c = I2c::new(p.I2C0, I2cConfig::default())
        .unwrap()
        .with_sda(p.GPIO8)
        .with_scl(p.GPIO9);

    let st = SystemTimer::new(p.SYSTIMER);
    let mut alarm = PeriodicTimer::new(st.alarm0);
    alarm.set_interrupt_handler(alarm_isr);
    alarm.start(Duration::from_millis(1000)).unwrap();
    alarm.listen();

    critical_section::with(|cs| {
        ALARM.replace(cs, Some(alarm));
    });

    loop {
        let tick = critical_section::with(|cs| {
            let v = *TICK_FLAG.borrow_ref(cs);
            TICK_FLAG.replace(cs, false);
            v
        });
        if tick {
            let mut buf = [0u8; 2];
            if i2c.write_read(TMP102_ADDR, &[0x00], &mut buf).is_ok() {
                let raw = ((buf[0] as i16) << 8) | (buf[1] as i16);
                let temp_c = (raw >> 4) as f32 * 0.0625;
                println!("T = {:.2} °C", temp_c);
                if temp_c > THRESHOLD_C {
                    led.set_high();
                } else {
                    led.set_low();
                }
            }
        }
        core::hint::spin_loop();
    }
}
  • [ ] Step 8.6: Exclude the example crate from the workspace

In the root Cargo.toml, append "examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102" to the exclude list (alphabetical):

exclude = [
    "crates/firmware-hal-test",
    "examples/esp32s3-blinky",
    "examples/esp32s3-hello-world",
    "examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102",
]
  • [ ] Step 8.7: Verify the firmware compiles with +esp toolchain

Run from the workspace root:

cargo +esp build --release --manifest-path examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/Cargo.toml 2>&1 | tail -10

Expected: Finished release profile [optimized + debuginfo] target(s) — produces examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/target/xtensa-esp32s3-none-elf/release/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102 ELF.

If the +esp toolchain is not installed, this step fails with error: toolchain 'esp' is not installed. In that case, install it first via espup install (one-time) per examples/esp32s3-blinky/README.md. Do not skip this step — the e2e test in task 9 depends on the ELF.

  • [ ] Step 8.8: Commit
git add examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/ Cargo.toml
git commit -m "feat(examples): esp32s3-i2c-tmp102 firmware (Plan 4 task 8)

esp-hal-based firmware that reads TMP102 over I2C0 once per second
via SYSTIMER alarm, prints temperature over USB-Serial-JTAG, and
toggles GPIO2 high when temperature exceeds 30 C.

Excluded from workspace (uses +esp toolchain), mirrors the layout

docs.title: Task 9: Sim end-to-end test
docs.text:
Files: - Create: crates/core/tests/e2e_i2c_tmp102.rs

  • [ ] Step 9.1: Write the e2e test

Create crates/core/tests/e2e_i2c_tmp102.rs:

// LabWired - Firmware Simulation Platform
// Copyright (C) 2026 Andrii Shylenko
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
//
// Plan 4: end-to-end test that builds examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102 and
// runs it in the simulator, asserting:
//   * JTAG stdout contains at least 4 "T = NN.NN °C" lines
//   * Temperatures are monotonically increasing (modulo 35→20 wrap)
//   * GPIO2 transitioned 0→1 at a cycle consistent with crossing 30 °C
//
// Gated on `--features esp32s3-fixtures` so plain `cargo test` (without
// the ESP toolchain) still works.

#![cfg(feature = "esp32s3-fixtures")]

use labwired_core::boot::esp32s3::{fast_boot, BootOpts};
use labwired_core::bus::SystemBus;
use labwired_core::peripherals::esp32s3::gpio::GpioObserver;
use labwired_core::system::xtensa::{configure_xtensa_esp32s3, Esp32s3Opts};
use labwired_core::{Cpu, SimulationError};
use std::path::PathBuf;
use std::process::Command;
use std::sync::{Arc, Mutex};

#[derive(Debug, Default)]
struct RecordingObserver {
    events: Mutex<Vec<(u8, bool, bool, u64)>>,
}

impl GpioObserver for RecordingObserver {
    fn on_pin_change(&self, pin: u8, from: bool, to: bool, sim_cycle: u64) {
        self.events.lock().unwrap().push((pin, from, to, sim_cycle));
    }
}

fn firmware_path() -> PathBuf {
    PathBuf::from(
        "../../examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/target/xtensa-esp32s3-none-elf/release/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102",
    )
}

fn ensure_firmware_built() -> PathBuf {
    let elf = firmware_path();
    let src = PathBuf::from("../../examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/src/main.rs");
    if elf.exists() {
        if let (Ok(elf_meta), Ok(src_meta)) = (std::fs::metadata(&elf), std::fs::metadata(&src)) {
            if elf_meta.modified().unwrap() >= src_meta.modified().unwrap() {
                return elf;
            }
        }
    }
    let status = Command::new("cargo")
        .args(["+esp", "build", "--release"])
        .current_dir("../../examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102")
        .status()
        .expect("cargo +esp build (is the ESP toolchain installed?)");
    assert!(status.success(), "esp32s3-i2c-tmp102 build failed");
    elf
}

#[test]
fn e2e_i2c_tmp102_demo_runs_in_simulator() {
    let elf = ensure_firmware_built();
    let elf_bytes = std::fs::read(&elf).expect("read ELF");

    let mut bus = SystemBus::new();
    let mut wiring = configure_xtensa_esp32s3(&mut bus, &Esp32s3Opts::default()).unwrap();

    let observer = Arc::new(RecordingObserver::default());
    let obs_dyn: Arc<dyn GpioObserver> = observer.clone();

    // Register the observer with the GPIO peripheral.
    for p in bus.peripherals.iter_mut() {
        if let Some(any) = p.dev.as_any_mut() {
            if let Some(gpio) =
                any.downcast_mut::<labwired_core::peripherals::esp32s3::gpio::Esp32s3Gpio>()
            {
                gpio.add_observer(obs_dyn.clone());
            }
        }
    }

    // Drain JTAG output via a Vec sink.
    let jtag = Arc::new(Mutex::new(Vec::<u8>::new()));
    for p in bus.peripherals.iter_mut() {
        if let Some(any) = p.dev.as_any_mut() {
            if let Some(uj) = any.downcast_mut::<
                labwired_core::peripherals::esp32s3::usb_serial_jtag::UsbSerialJtag,
            >() {
                uj.set_sink(Some(jtag.clone()));
            }
        }
    }

    let mut cpu = fast_boot(
        &mut bus,
        &elf_bytes,
        &BootOpts {
            stack_top_fallback: 0x3FCD_FFF0,
            icache_backing: Some(wiring.icache_backing.take().unwrap()),
            dcache_backing: Some(wiring.dcache_backing.take().unwrap()),
        },
    )
    .expect("fast_boot");

    // Run for ~5 s of simulated time at 80 MHz = 400 M cycles. The blinky
    // e2e uses ~1 M steps; for I2C we need at least 5 alarm ticks → boost.
    let cfg = labwired_core::SimulationConfig::default();
    let mut steps = 0usize;
    while steps < 8_000_000 {
        match cpu.step(&mut bus, &[], &cfg) {
            Ok(()) => steps += 1,
            Err(SimulationError::BreakpointHit(_)) => break,
            Err(e) => panic!("simulator error: {e:?}"),
        }
    }

    // Decode JTAG bytes → text and split lines.
    let text = String::from_utf8_lossy(&jtag.lock().unwrap()).to_string();
    let temp_lines: Vec<&str> = text
        .lines()
        .filter(|l| l.starts_with("T = "))
        .collect();
    assert!(
        temp_lines.len() >= 4,
        "expected ≥ 4 'T = ...' lines; got {}: {text:?}",
        temp_lines.len()
    );

    // Parse temperatures and verify drift direction (monotonic until wrap).
    let temps: Vec<f32> = temp_lines
        .iter()
        .filter_map(|l| {
            l.strip_prefix("T = ")
                .and_then(|rest| rest.split(' ').next())
                .and_then(|n| n.parse::<f32>().ok())
        })
        .collect();
    assert!(temps.len() >= 4, "could not parse temperatures: {temp_lines:?}");
    for window in temps.windows(2) {
        // Allow a single wrap (35→20) in the run; otherwise monotonic up.
        if window[1] >= window[0] {
            // monotonic up — ok
        } else {
            assert!(
                window[1] < 21.0 && window[0] > 34.0,
                "non-monotonic without wrap: {} → {}",
                window[0],
                window[1]
            );
        }
    }

    // GPIO2 must have transitioned 0→1 at least once after the threshold.
    let events = observer.events.lock().unwrap();
    let has_rising = events.iter().any(|&(pin, from, to, _)| pin == 2 && !from && to);
    assert!(
        has_rising,
        "expected GPIO2 to rise (0→1) at some point; got events: {events:?}"
    );
}
  • [ ] Step 9.2: Run the test (will trigger firmware build first time)

Run: cargo test -p labwired-core --features esp32s3-fixtures --test e2e_i2c_tmp102 2>&1 | tail -20

Expected (first run): firmware build via cargo +esp build, then test PASS.

If the test fails on JTAG output: re-run with stdout passthrough to see captured bytes:

cargo test -p labwired-core --features esp32s3-fixtures --test e2e_i2c_tmp102 -- --nocapture 2>&1 | tail -40
  • [ ] Step 9.3: Verify the existing blinky/hello-world e2e tests still pass

Run: cargo test -p labwired-core --features esp32s3-fixtures 2>&1 | grep -E "test result|FAILED" | tail -10

Expected: all e2e tests PASS (e2e_blinky, e2e_hello_world, e2e_i2c_tmp102).

  • [ ] Step 9.4: Commit
git add crates/core/tests/e2e_i2c_tmp102.rs
git commit -m "test(esp32s3): e2e I2C+TMP102 demo (Plan 4 task 9)

Builds the esp32s3-i2c-tmp102 firmware, boots it in the simulator,
and asserts:
  - JTAG output contains >= 4 'T = NN.NN C' lines
  - temperatures drift monotonically (allowing one 35->20 wrap)
  - GPIO2 transitions 0->1 at the threshold crossing

Gated on --features esp32s3-fixtures, mirroring e2e_blinky."

docs.title: Task 10: HW oracle — controller-register-level tests
docs.text:
Dependency: This task assumes the hw-oracle harness has already merged to main (PR #82). If it hasn't, run tasks 1-9 + 11-12 first and revisit this task once #82 lands.

The hw-oracle harness runs OracleCase programs by either parsing .word directives (OracleCase::asm) or loading a pre-built ELF (OracleCase::elf). Real Xtensa mnemonics need to be assembled with xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as and linked with the existing fixtures/xtensa-asm/linker.ld, then loaded as ELF fixtures. We follow the same pattern that fibonacci.elf already uses.

Files: - Create: fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_empty_cmdlist.s - Create: fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.s - Create: fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_fifo_pushes.s - Modify: fixtures/xtensa-asm/Makefile (add new ELF targets) - Create: crates/hw-oracle/tests/i2c_oracles.rs

  • [ ] Step 10.1: Write the assembly fixture for "empty command list → TRANS_COMPLETE"

Create fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_empty_cmdlist.s:

    .section .text.entry, "ax"
    .align 4
    .global _start
_start:
    /* I2C0_BASE = 0x6001_3000.
     * Set CMD0 = END (opcode 4 → word 0x2000), then CTR = TRANS_START
     * (bit 5 → 0x20). Both writes go through DRAM-aliased I2C bank
     * accesses, which on real silicon translate to peripheral writes.
     */
    l32r    a2, i2c0_base       /* a2 = 0x6001_3000               */
    l32r    a3, cmd_end          /* a3 = 0x0000_2000 (END opcode)  */
    s32i    a3, a2, 0x58         /* CMD0 = END                     */
    l32r    a3, ctr_trans_start  /* a3 = 0x0000_0020               */
    s32i    a3, a2, 0x04         /* CTR = TRANS_START              */
    /* Read INT_RAW so the captured-mem step sees a stable value.   */
    l32i    a4, a2, 0x20
    break   1, 15

    .align 4
i2c0_base:        .word 0x60013000
cmd_end:          .word 0x00002000
ctr_trans_start:  .word 0x00000020
  • [ ] Step 10.2: Write the assembly fixture for "unmatched addr → NACK"

Create fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.s:

    .section .text.entry, "ax"
    .align 4
    .global _start
_start:
    l32r    a2, i2c0_base       /* a2 = 0x6001_3000               */

    /* CMD0 = RSTART (opcode 0) */
    movi    a3, 0
    s32i    a3, a2, 0x58

    /* CMD1 = WRITE 1 byte (opcode 1, byte_num=1 → 0x0801)        */
    l32r    a3, cmd_write_1
    s32i    a3, a2, 0x5C

    /* CMD2 = STOP (opcode 3 → 0x1800) */
    l32r    a3, cmd_stop
    s32i    a3, a2, 0x60

    /* TX FIFO ← 0xA0 (addr 0x50 << 1, no slave at this addr)      */
    movi    a3, 0xA0
    s32i    a3, a2, 0x18

    /* CTR = TRANS_START (0x20) */
    l32r    a3, ctr_trans_start
    s32i    a3, a2, 0x04

    /* Capture INT_RAW into a4 (memory snapshot reads it via OpenOCD) */
    l32i    a4, a2, 0x20
    break   1, 15

    .align 4
i2c0_base:        .word 0x60013000
cmd_write_1:      .word 0x00000801
cmd_stop:         .word 0x00001800
ctr_trans_start:  .word 0x00000020
  • [ ] Step 10.3: Write the assembly fixture for "8 TX FIFO pushes → FIFO_ST level 8"

Create fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_fifo_pushes.s:

    .section .text.entry, "ax"
    .align 4
    .global _start
_start:
    l32r    a2, i2c0_base       /* a2 = 0x6001_3000   */
    movi    a4, 8                /* loop counter        */
push_loop:
    movi    a3, 0xAA
    s32i    a3, a2, 0x18         /* TX FIFO push        */
    addi    a4, a4, -1
    bnez    a4, push_loop
    /* Capture FIFO_ST into a5 */
    l32i    a5, a2, 0x44
    break   1, 15

    .align 4
i2c0_base:        .word 0x60013000
  • [ ] Step 10.4: Add Makefile targets for the new ELFs

Modify fixtures/xtensa-asm/Makefile. Add the new ELFs to the all: target and the per-ELF rules. The existing fibonacci pattern is the template:

TOOL = $(HOME)/.rustup/toolchains/esp/xtensa-esp-elf/esp-15.2.0_20250920/xtensa-esp-elf/bin
AS       = $(TOOL)/xtensa-esp32s3-elf-as
LD       = $(TOOL)/xtensa-esp32s3-elf-ld
OBJCOPY  = $(TOOL)/xtensa-esp32s3-elf-objcopy
OBJDUMP  = $(TOOL)/xtensa-esp32s3-elf-objdump

ELFS = fibonacci.elf \
       i2c0_empty_cmdlist.elf \
       i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.elf \
       i2c0_fifo_pushes.elf

all: $(ELFS) fibonacci.bin

fibonacci.elf: fibonacci.s linker.ld
    $(AS) --no-transform $< -o fibonacci.o
    $(LD) -T linker.ld fibonacci.o -o $@

i2c0_empty_cmdlist.elf: i2c0_empty_cmdlist.s linker.ld
    $(AS) --no-transform $< -o i2c0_empty_cmdlist.o
    $(LD) -T linker.ld i2c0_empty_cmdlist.o -o $@

i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.elf: i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.s linker.ld
    $(AS) --no-transform $< -o i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.o
    $(LD) -T linker.ld i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.o -o $@

i2c0_fifo_pushes.elf: i2c0_fifo_pushes.s linker.ld
    $(AS) --no-transform $< -o i2c0_fifo_pushes.o
    $(LD) -T linker.ld i2c0_fifo_pushes.o -o $@

fibonacci.bin: fibonacci.elf
    $(OBJCOPY) -O binary $< $@

disasm: fibonacci.elf
    $(OBJDUMP) -d $<

clean:
    rm -f *.o *.elf *.bin

.PHONY: all disasm clean
  • [ ] Step 10.5: Build the new ELFs

Run from the repo root:

make -C fixtures/xtensa-asm 2>&1 | tail -10

Expected: three new ELFs land in fixtures/xtensa-asm/: - i2c0_empty_cmdlist.elf - i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.elf - i2c0_fifo_pushes.elf

If the toolchain path differs from ~/.rustup/toolchains/esp/xtensa-esp-elf/esp-15.2.0_20250920/, adjust the TOOL variable in the Makefile.

Verify the disassembly looks right:

~/.rustup/toolchains/esp/xtensa-esp-elf/esp-15.2.0_20250920/xtensa-esp-elf/bin/xtensa-esp32s3-elf-objdump -d fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_empty_cmdlist.elf 2>&1 | head -20

Expected: _start at 0x4037_0000 containing the l32r/s32i/break sequence.

  • [ ] Step 10.6: Write the oracle test wrapper

Create crates/hw-oracle/tests/i2c_oracles.rs:

// LabWired - Firmware Simulation Platform
// Copyright (C) 2026 Andrii Shylenko
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
//
// Plan 4 L3: ESP32-S3 I2C0 controller-register-level oracle tests.
//
// Each test loads a pre-built fixture ELF, runs it on real silicon (via
// OpenOCD) AND in the simulator, then diffs the resulting INT_RAW or
// FIFO_ST value captured into IRAM-aliased memory. They do NOT require
// a physical TMP102 — only the I2C0 controller itself.
//
// Built ELFs live in fixtures/xtensa-asm/. See the Makefile.

use labwired_hw_oracle::{hw_oracle_test, OracleCase};

const I2C0_BASE: u32 = 0x6001_3000;
const REG_INT_RAW: u32 = 0x20;
const REG_FIFO_ST: u32 = 0x44;
const INT_TRANS_COMPLETE: u32 = 1 << 7;
const INT_NACK: u32 = 1 << 11;

fn fixture(name: &str) -> String {
    // Match the convention used by the existing fibonacci_10 oracle test:
    // anchor the path at the manifest dir so it works regardless of CWD.
    format!(
        "{}/../../fixtures/xtensa-asm/{name}.elf",
        env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR")
    )
}

/// Empty command list (CMD0 = END) → INT_RAW.TRANS_COMPLETE set.
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn i2c0_empty_command_list_sets_trans_complete() -> OracleCase {
    OracleCase::elf(&fixture("i2c0_empty_cmdlist"))
        .capture_mem(&[I2C0_BASE + REG_INT_RAW])
        .expect(|st| {
            let int_raw = st.read_mem(I2C0_BASE + REG_INT_RAW);
            assert_eq!(
                int_raw & INT_TRANS_COMPLETE,
                INT_TRANS_COMPLETE,
                "TRANS_COMPLETE expected; got INT_RAW=0x{int_raw:08X}"
            );
        })
}

/// Unmatched address → INT_RAW.NACK set.
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn i2c0_unmatched_address_sets_nack() -> OracleCase {
    OracleCase::elf(&fixture("i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack"))
        .capture_mem(&[I2C0_BASE + REG_INT_RAW])
        .expect(|st| {
            let int_raw = st.read_mem(I2C0_BASE + REG_INT_RAW);
            assert_eq!(
                int_raw & INT_NACK,
                INT_NACK,
                "NACK expected; got INT_RAW=0x{int_raw:08X}"
            );
        })
}

/// 8 TX FIFO pushes → FIFO_ST.tx_level == 8.
#[hw_oracle_test]
fn i2c0_fifo_levels_after_8_pushes() -> OracleCase {
    OracleCase::elf(&fixture("i2c0_fifo_pushes"))
        .capture_mem(&[I2C0_BASE + REG_FIFO_ST])
        .expect(|st| {
            let fifo_st = st.read_mem(I2C0_BASE + REG_FIFO_ST);
            assert_eq!(
                fifo_st & 0x1F,
                8,
                "TX FIFO level should be 8; got 0x{fifo_st:08X}"
            );
        })
}

The #[hw_oracle_test] macro emits _sim, _hw, and _diff variants. The _sim variant runs unconditionally (always); _hw and _diff are #[ignore]'d unless --features hw-oracle is set.

  • [ ] Step 10.7: Run the sim-side variants

Run: cargo test -p labwired-hw-oracle --test i2c_oracles 2>&1 | tail -10

Expected: 3 tests PASS (i2c0_empty_command_list_sets_trans_complete_sim, i2c0_unmatched_address_sets_nack_sim, i2c0_fifo_levels_after_8_pushes_sim).

  • [ ] Step 10.8: Verify hw-oracle gated tests compile

Run: cargo check -p labwired-hw-oracle --tests --features hw-oracle 2>&1 | tail -5

Expected: compiles clean. The _hw and _diff variants only run when the feature is enabled and a board is attached on the runner.

  • [ ] Step 10.9: Commit
git add fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_empty_cmdlist.s \
        fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.s \
        fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_fifo_pushes.s \
        fixtures/xtensa-asm/Makefile \
        fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_empty_cmdlist.elf \
        fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_unmatched_addr_nack.elf \
        fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_fifo_pushes.elf \
        crates/hw-oracle/tests/i2c_oracles.rs
git commit -m "test(hw-oracle): I2C0 controller-register-level oracle bank (Plan 4 task 10)

Three oracle tests validate sim/HW parity at the I2C0 register-write
level without requiring a physical TMP102:

  1. CMD0=END + TRANS_START → INT_RAW.TRANS_COMPLETE set
  2. RSTART; WRITE 1; STOP with no slave at the address → INT_RAW.NACK set
  3. 8 TX FIFO pushes → FIFO_ST.tx_level == 8

Each test runs a pre-built Xtensa ELF (fixtures/xtensa-asm/i2c0_*.elf)
that pokes I2C0 registers and BREAKs. The hw-oracle harness diffs the
captured INT_RAW / FIFO_ST value between sim and real silicon."

docs.title: Task 11: README + RUNBOOK
docs.text:
Files: - Create: examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/README.md - Create: examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/RUNBOOK.md

  • [ ] Step 11.1: Write the README

Create examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/README.md:

# esp32s3-i2c-tmp102 — Plan 4 demo

ESP32-S3 firmware that reads a TMP102 temperature sensor over I²C0 once
per second, prints the temperature over USB-Serial-JTAG, and toggles
GPIO2 when temperature exceeds 30 °C.

## What it does

Each second the SYSTIMER alarm fires. The handler sets a flag; the main
loop then issues `I2c::write_read(0x48, &[0x00], &mut buf[2])`, decodes
a 12-bit temperature from the two-byte response, prints `T = NN.NN °C`,
and sets GPIO2 high if T > 30 °C, otherwise low.

The simulator's TMP102 model drifts +0.5 °C per read, wrapping from 35
back to 20 — so over ~30 reads the temperature sweeps through the
threshold both directions, exercising both edges of the GPIO toggle.

## Run in the simulator
cargo build --release --manifest-path examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/Cargo.toml labwired run \ --chip configs/chips/esp32s3-zero.yaml \ --firmware examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/target/xtensa-esp32s3-none-elf/release/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102 \ --max-steps 8000000 
Expected output:
T = 25.00 °C T = 25.50 °C T = 26.00 °C ... T = 30.00 °C T = 30.50 °C ← GPIO2 goes high here T = 31.00 °C ... 
The simulator also emits GPIO transition events to the configured tracer.

## Run on real hardware

See [RUNBOOK.md](RUNBOOK.md).

## Sources

- ESP32-S3 TRM v1.4 §29 (I²C controller)
- TI TMP102 datasheet (December 2008, Rev. June 2015)
- esp-hal 1.1.0 `i2c::master::I2c`

  • [ ] Step 11.2: Write the runbook

Create examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/RUNBOOK.md:

# Runbook — esp32s3-i2c-tmp102 on real hardware (Plan 4 L4)

Manual procedure to validate the firmware against a real ESP32-S3-Zero
with a physical TMP102 sensor attached. **Not** part of CI.

## Hardware

- ESP32-S3-Zero board (USB ID `303a:1001`)
- TMP102 breakout (e.g. SparkFun, Adafruit, generic AliExpress)
- 4 jumper wires
- USB-C cable

## Wiring

| TMP102 pin | ESP32-S3-Zero pin |
|------------|-------------------|
| SDA        | GPIO8             |
| SCL        | GPIO9             |
| V+         | 3V3               |
| GND        | GND               |
| ADD0       | GND (selects address 0x48) |

The TMP102 is 1.4-3.6 V tolerant, so direct 3.3 V from the dev board is fine.
No external pull-ups needed at 100 kHz I²C — esp-hal enables the internal pulls.

## Flash + monitor

From the workspace root:
cargo +esp run --release --manifest-path examples/esp32s3-i2c-tmp102/Cargo.toml 
`cargo run` invokes `espflash flash --monitor` per `.cargo/config.toml`,
flashing the binary and tailing JTAG output.

## Acceptance

The serial monitor should show one line per second:
T = 23.50 °C ← real ambient T = 23.50 °C T = 23.62 °C ... 
If you breathe on the TMP102 you should see the temperature jump up.
Once it crosses 30 °C, GPIO2 goes high (probe with a multimeter or LED).

## Common failures

- **No output at all.** Check `lsusb | grep 303a:1001`. If the board
  is not detected, replug the USB cable. Also check that no other
  process holds the JTAG (`fuser /dev/ttyACM0`).
- **`I2C bus error` panics in JTAG output.** Check wiring; confirm
  pull-ups are present (some breakouts include them, some don't).
- **All temperatures clamp at -1.0 °C.** TMP102 ADD0 is floating; tie
  it to GND.

README documents what the demo does and how to run it in the simulator.
RUNBOOK covers the L4 manual procedure on real hardware: wiring a
TMP102 to the ESP32-S3-Zero, flashing with espflash, and the expected
output. Not part of CI."

Spec section Implementing task(s) Tmp102 device, drift, wrap, pointer Tasks 1, 2 I²C0 controller register file (CTR, SLAVE_ADDR, FIFO_DATA, FIFO_CONF, INT_*, FIFO_ST, CMD0..CMD15) Task 3 Command-list executor (RSTART, STOP, END terminators, TRANS_START auto-clear) Task 4 FIFO_DATA word-read RX pop Task 5 WRITE/READ command execution + slave attachment + NACK Task 6 configure_xtensa_esp32s3 wiring + chip yaml Task 7 Firmware crate (esp-hal, SYSTIMER, I²C, JTAG, GPIO threshold) Task 8 Sim e2e test with JTAG+GPIO assertions Task 9 L3 hw-oracle controller-register-level tests Task 10 README + RUNBOOK (L4 manual) Task 11 Workspace integration / final pass Task 12

All six pieces from the spec's "Architecture" section are covered. All four testing layers (L1 unit / L2 sim e2e / L3 hw-oracle / L4 manual runbook) are covered.

docs.title: Risks tracked from the spec
docs.text:
• Command-list semantics drift between sim and HW → Task 10's L3 oracle bank.
  • esp-hal version pinning → Task 8's Cargo.toml pins esp-hal 1.1.0 explicitly.
  • TMP102 pointer reset on RSTART → Task 1 implements pointer-set-on-first-write-after-start; Task 2 doesn't change this; the test pointer_set_by_first_write_after_start proves it.
  • First-peripheral hw-oracle pattern → Task 10 keeps the bank to 3 register-level tests; richer peripheral oracles deferred per the spec.
  • NACK behavior on real HW vs sim with attached TMP102 → Task 6 adds NACK when no slave matches; the sim's attached TMP102 ACKs by default. The L3 oracle test #2 specifically exercises the no-slave path.

docs.title: ESP32-S3 ROM Auto-Provisioning + Faithful Default Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For agentic workers: REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (- [ ]) syntax for tracking.

Goal: Make the ESP32-S3 boot the real Espressif ROM by default — auto-discovering and extracting the ROM blob from the installed toolchain — with the thunk path kept only as a labelled no-blob fallback, and delete the dead wifi_thunks module.

Architecture: A new Rust module ports scripts/make_esp32s3_rom_bins.py (ELF → flat IROM/DROM images via goblin), discovers the toolchain ROM ELF, caches the extracted images keyed on the ELF content hash, and feeds them into configure_xtensa_esp32s3. The existing LABWIRED_ESP32S3_ROM/_DROM env vars still override; if nothing resolves, the model falls back to the thunk harness. A boot_mode field on Esp32s3Wiring records which path was taken.

Tech Stack: Rust, goblin (already a labwired-core dep), the existing boot:: and system::xtensa modules.

Spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-05-esp32s3-full-chip-model-design.md (slices S1 + S2).

Scope note: This plan is slices S1+S2 only. Slice S3 (SVD coverage tool + faithful-mode gate) and the per-peripheral register/FSM-completeness slices (S4+) are separate plans.
